observations.recommendation:
Prioritize the reconciliation of balances between the accounting records and RPCPPE for fair presentation of PPE accounts in the Financial Statements; and

After reconciliation, Management should consider disposing the unserviceable, request from COA for the derecognition of non-existing and missing PPEs from the Agency’s book of accounts, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, otherwise known as the Guidelines and Procedures in the Conduct of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), Recognition of PPE Items Found at Station, and Disposition for Non-existing/Missing PPE Items, for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances of Government Agencies.
observations.reason: Unreconciled balances of PPE account between Accounting and GSO still exist. Request for Disposal was not yet made by the LGU. However, the Accounting Office and GSO explained that the discrepancies are primarily due to the use of net book values in accounting records versus historical costs in GSO records. Nevertheless, both offices are working closely to fully reconcile the differences.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Management require the Municipal Accountant to trace the expenditures for road networks in CYs 2016-2018 that were recorded in various PPE accounts and to reclassify thereof in the Road Networks account.

Also, we reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant, GSO and Municipal Engineer to exert efforts in locating documents and information pertaining to the road networks of the LGU for CY 2015 and prior years to properly recognize the same in the LGU’s Financial Statements for a fair presentation of the PPE account; and

Direct the above-mentioned key officials to accomplish the duties and responsibilities assigned to them.
observations.managementAction: Implemented | The concerned key officials and offices accomplished the duties and responsibilities assigned to them for the implementation of the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: No adjustments were made by the Accounting Office, and local road networks from CY 2015 and prior years remain unrecorded. Management explained that no road networks were constructed or completed during those years. However, they committed to locating and reviewing relevant documents to support this claim and to address this recurring audit finding.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and take up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are adjusted on timely basis; and

Perform constant review of transactions to deter error and omissions.
observations.reason: Unreconciled balance still exists. The Accounting Office attributed the discrepancies to prior years’ balances, which are difficult to trace due to unavailability of prior years’ records. Nevertheless, they reported ongoing efforts to reconcile the differences and have already identified several reconciling items.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to identify the cause or reason for the deficit or cash overdraft. Thereafter, the Municipal Accountant should carry out the necessary actions to ensure that Trust Liabilities-DRMMF have a sufficient cash back-up.
observations.reason: A deficit/     overdraft still exists. However, management has committed to review prior years’ transactions that contributed to the deficit and to reconcile the affected accounts.

Significant delinquencies still exist. The LGU explained that Real Property Taxes (RPT) remain partially uncollected due to various issues faced by property owners, including restrictions on certain properties. The Municipal Treasurer added that the Province of Isabela is not yet availing of the available remedies, such as administrative action through levy or judicial proceedings. Nonetheless, management expressed their commitment to developing additional strategies to improve collection efforts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management ensure that all budgeted projects, programs and activities are optimally and fully implemented to timely bring the desired benefits to the intended users. For PPAs that were still unimplemented positive efforts should be made to fast track the completion of the said PPAs.
observations.reason: As of the end of CY 2024, four (4) budgeted PPAs as indicated in the LGU’s LDRRMFIP remained unimplemented. As a result, the allocated funds remained idle, and the intended purposes were not fully achieved.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following to Management:
The Municipal Accountant to perform a detailed reconciliation of records of the cash accounts and draw adjusting/correcting entries as necessary; and
The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to periodically reconcile their records and update the same to reflect an accurate report.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to take up the necessary adjusting entries in their books of accounts to present fairly the balances of the PPE and other affected accounts.
We also recommended that the Supply Officer prepare and maintain the prescribed forms, registries, and reports, as required in the Circular, to strengthen controls over that agency’s semi-expendable properties.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management:
Prioritize the reconciliation of balances between the accounting records and RPCPPE for fair presentation of PPE accounts in the Financial Statements; and
After reconciliation, consider disposing the unserviceable, request from COA for the derecognition of non-existing and missing PPEs from the Agency’s book of accounts, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, otherwise known as the Guidelines and Procedures in the Conduct of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), Recognition of PPE Items Found at Station, and Disposition for Non-existing/Missing PPE Items, for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances of Government Agencies.

We recommended the following to Management:
Adjust the Loans Payable- Domestic account to reflect the total loan obligation as per the bank’s confirmation;
Reconcile the LGU’s records and the bank’s confirmation and the loan-related documents to ensure accurate financial reporting; and
Enhance internal controls and accounting procedures to prevent similar discrepancies in the future loan transactions.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Perform a thorough reconciliation between the subsidiary ledgers and the general ledgers to identify and resolve discrepancies;
Examine source documents, journal entries, and transactions to verify the accuracy of recorded amounts in both ledgers and make necessary adjusting entries, if any; and
Regularly update both ledgers to reflect accurate and complete transactions, preventing future discrepancies.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and take up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are adjusted on timely basis; and
Perform constant review of transactions to deter errors and omissions.

However, we recommended the following to Management:
Ensure that PPAs funded out of the 20% DF are implemented within the period specified in the AIP. For PPAs that were still unimplemented positive efforts should be made to fast track the completion of the said PPAs; and
Submit justification for not being able to implement the following projects from the current and continuing appropriations:
Amortization of Loan with DBP (1. Completion of Multi-Purpose Building and 2. Construction of Sanitary Landfill);
Installation of Reinforced Concrete Pipe Culvert (RCPC) going to Small Water Impounding Project (SWIP) Ayangan Dapis, Sto. Niño; and
Improvement of the comfort room for Persons with Disabilities (PWDs) at the LGU.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to exert further efforts in determining the root cause of the deficit or cash overdraft. Subsequently, appropriate measures should be undertaken to ensure that the Trust Liabilities- DRRMF are adequately supported by corresponding cash balances.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure that all budgeted projects, programs and activities are optimally and fully implemented to timely bring the desired benefits to the intended users. For PPAs that were still unimplemented positive efforts should be made to fast track the completion of the said PPAs; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to immediately revert back the unexpended balance of the Special Trust account to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund, pending the actual transfer of cash to the General Fund account and ensure that the above entries should always be made annually before the preparation of year-end financial statements.

We recommended the following to Management:
To use the template/format specified in the Annex A (LDRRMFIP) and B (Report on Utilization of Disaster Risk reduction and Management Fund) of COA Circular No. 2012-002 for effective monitoring of the fund; and
The LDRRMC include in their plan the utilization of the unexpended LDRRM Fund balances from prior years, and specify the list of PPAs to be charged against the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years in its LDRRMFIP prior to the utilization of the same pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended the following to Management:
Conduct personnel audit, review, assessment and evaluation of existing manpower complement to prepare an updated organization structure/pattern;
Remove excessive and unnecessary JOs who are performing redundant functions with other workers; and
Formulate uniform policy and procedures in engaging and managing outsourced workers taking into consideration the issuances of Civil Service Commission as to the appropriate forms and type of contracts and classification of workers.

We commended Management for registering a positive collection efficiency rate for Business Taxes during the year 2024.
However, we reiterated our recommendations that Management:
Conduct more extensive tax campaign so that landowners are apprised of the advantages/services that the Municipality would render if taxes are paid;
Develop an efficient and effective monitoring and collection system so as to intensify the collection of its outstanding RPT/SET Receivables; and
Avail of all the possible remedies provided under the Rules and Regulations Implementing the Local Government Code of 1991 to enforce the collection of RPT.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Treasurer to (i) deposit the collections intact and on time as
mandated in the afore-cited provisions of R.A. No. 7160 and NGAS
Manual Volume I; (ii) ensure that original copies of validated deposit slips
are properly attached to support the transactions to ascertain the
authenticity of the documents; and (iii) regularly update the cashbooks
and require all accountable officers to do the same to ensure accurate
recording, monitoring, and reconciliation of financial transactions; and
b. Municipal Accountant to implement appropriate measures to ensure that
collections and deposits are properly recorded, to fairly present the
accurate balance of cash accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require all concerned officials and employees to strictly comply with the
regulations on the liquidation of cash advances as provided in Section 5 of
COA Circular No. 97-002; and
b. instruct the Municipal Accountant to strengthen internal control
procedures over the management of cash advances by establishing and
maintaining a monitoring system to track the issuance and liquidation
status of all cash advances, and to ensure that no additional cash advances
are granted to any officer or employee with outstanding or unliquidated
balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Supply/Property Officer to immediately prepare and submit the
corresponding SSMI to the Accounting Office to facilitate the proper
recording of all issued supplies and materials as expenses; and
b. Municipal Accountant to (i) prepare the necessary schedules and Supplies
Ledger Cards for each inventory item and periodically reconcile with the
stock cards prepared by the supply officer; and (ii) coordinate with the
Supply/Property Officer to trace the unreconciled balances and make the
appropriate adjustments, if warranted, to ensure the completeness and
accuracy of the reported balances of inventory at year end.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct the Inventory Committee to (i) conduct comprehensive physical
count of all properties annually, ensuring that all assets are properly
identified and counted; (ii) reconcile the results of the physical count with
the accounting records to ensure accuracy and completeness; (iii) submit
the reconciled reports to the Audit Team in accordance with Section 124,
Volume I of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs; and (iv) report all the
unserviceable properties in the IIRUP supported with proper
documentation for subsequent disposal of the same in accordance with
COA rules and regulations;
b. instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) ascertain and analyze the
breakdown/composition of the unidentified PPE balances totaling
P4,293,049.79, for the reconciliation of records and as basis for making the
necessary adjustments; (ii) prepare the necessary schedules and
subsidiary ledger of each PPE including the CIP accounts; (iii) review the
schedule of depreciation and prepare necessary journal entries to record
adjustments for the unrecorded depreciation; (iv) re-assess the provided
useful life of each PPE to reflect the actual status and valuations; and (v)
prepare and maintain the prescribed property ledger cards and subsidiary
records for Local Road Networks and disclose the information for the said
property in the Notes to Financial Statements in accordance with the
provision cited under COA Circular No. 2015-008;
c. require the Municipal Treasurer to (i) maintain and regularly update the
Property Cards for each item of PPE; (ii) take charge of the disposal of
unserviceable properties pursuant to Sections 163 and 167 of the Rules
and Regulations on Supply and Property Management; and (iii) facilitate
the preparation of transfer documents of PPE intended for donation; and
d. Direct the Municipal Engineer to (i) coordinate with the Municipal
Accountant with regards to the confirmation of status of each PPAs; and
(ii) provide the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer with the
complete description and cost segregation of road components for road
projects for proper disclosure.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal
Treasurer and Municipal Accountant investigate the discrepancies noted between
the book balance and the loan balance per confirmation amounting to P55,303.11,
as well as the P1,005,645.75 deviation from the amortization schedule. Further,
we recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting journal
entries, if necessary, and ensure that the Loans Payable are properly classified in
the financial statements.

We therefore recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer cause the immediate refund of
the unexpended balances of completed projects to the respective source agencies
or National Treasury pursuant to Item 4.9 of COA Circular No. 94-013 and Item
5.0 of DBM Circular Letter No. 2021-13.
Further, for the collections representing shares of NGAs, we recommended
that the Municipal Treasurer implement appropriate measures to ensure the
timely remittance of these funds. Additionally, these collections should be
properly recorded and maintained under the Trust Fund account in compliance
with Section 309(b) of R.A. No. 7160.

We therefore recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the
Municipal Treasurer to (i) ensure that all Accountable Officers have active and
sufficient fidelity bonds before performing any duties involving the handling of
collections or public funds, and that such bonds are renewed at least 30 days prior
to their expiration to avoid any lapse in coverage; (ii) ensure that no cash advances
or collections are handled by officers whose bond coverage is insufficient or
exceeds their maximum allowable cash accountability; and (iii) submit certified
copies of all approved bonds, including renewal and cancellation documents to the
Audit Team for proper verification and audit documentation in compliance with
Section 10, Chapter II of COA Memorandum No. 2013-004.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC to (i) submit
written explanation on the above-noted lapses/deficiencies; (ii) carefully evaluate
the documentary requirements of the participating bidder/s during post-
qualification to ascertain the accuracy of the information contained therein as well
as the completeness of the supporting documents attached to the statements; (iii)
ensure that the ITB, NOA, NTP and contracts are posted in the PhilGEPS website
within the prescribed period as well as in the agency’s website or social media
platforms to enhance transparency and public monitoring; (iv) see to it that at
least two observers in addition to COA representative are invited to the
procurement activities and that proof of receipt of their invitations is properly
documented and attached; (v) notify all other participating bidders in writing of
the bidding results and ensure that proof of such notification is properly
documented and attached; (vi) ensure that maximum period for each
procurement activity is observed to avoid delays in the implementation; and (vii)
strictly observe the prescribed period provided in COA Circular No. 2009-001 in
the submission of contracts/POs and their supporting documents to the Audit
Team.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Engineer to furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the approved suspension and
time extension for the two projects implemented beyond their respective contract
periods.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) endeavor to implement projects
immediately for the benefit of the general public; and (b) instruct the MPDC and
Municipal Engineer to (i) ensure that preliminary detailed engineering activities
are properly carried out prior to implementation to ascertain the viability of the
PPAs; (ii) closely monitor project timelines, taking corrective actions for any
delays, to guarantee completion thereof strictly in accordance within its target;
and (b) re-program and/or re-align the appropriation for PPAs which are no
longer viable for implementation.
Further, we reiterate our previous recommendation that the MDC properly
identify the expected outputs of all PPAs in the AIP before its implementation to
facilitate prompt validation.
Lastly, we recommended that the BAC submit the documents related to the
Improvement of Tourism Site project to the Municipal Accountant for proper
recording. The procurement documents should likewise be submitted to the Audit
Team for review and evaluation.

a. LDRRMO to (i) conduct a thorough evaluation of each proposed project
and clearly determine the expected output to ensure their viability prior to
its implementation; (ii) closely monitor the implementation, take corrective
actions for any delays and initiate the re-programming and/or re-alignment
of the appropriation for PPAs which are no longer feasible for
implementation to ensure optimum utilization of the Fund; (iii) review the
expenditures charged to the LDRRMF, particularly those under continuing
appropriation, to avoid commission of any significant errors that might
impair the legality and regularity of the transactions; and (iv) ensure that
all PPAs to be charged against the LDRRMF are incorporated in the
LDRRMFIP and AIP;
b. Municipal Accountant to (i) require the submission of LDRRMFIP for the
expenditures charged to the continuing appropriation and thoroughly
check the procedures to ensure propriety and regularity of the transactions;
(ii) make necessary adjustments to correct the recording of the expenditures
charged to the continuing appropriation totaling P305,570.00; (iii) ensure
the proper recording of the transfer of the unexpended balances of QRF
and MF-MOOE; and (iv) exert effort to trace the difference and reconcile
the respective balances of the Cash in Bank balance and unutilized DRRMF
transferred to the STF to fully ascertain the correct amount of balances to
be reported to present fair presentation of the financial statements at year-
end.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Chairperson, GPFS- TWG)/GAD Focal Person to (i) prepare a revised
GPB, if necessary, to accommodate changes in the original GPB after the
receipt of the approved/enacted Annual and Supplemental Budgets and
inform the concerned DILG Office in writing of the changes made thereto
pursuant to Item 6 (C) (7.2) of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No.
2016-01; (ii) see to it that PPAs are feasible and properly planned for
implementation within the budget year to ensure the optimum utilization
of funds and support the full realization of GAD objectives; (iii) ensure that
expenditures are accurately reported in the AR and include the actions
taken by the LGU on the COA audit findings and recommendations in
accordance with Item 4.1(C)(8.5) of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC
No. 2013-01; (iv) ensure that PPAs undergo the required procurement
procedures as prescribed under Section 10, Rule IV and Section 48.1, Rule
XVI of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184; and (v) coordinate with the
Municipal Accountant on the submission of the necessary documents to
substantiate the expenditures.
b. Municipal Treasurer to (i) submit written explanation on the noted
disbursements which were processed despite lack of signature by the
Municipal Accountant; (ii) ensure that all disbursements are properly
reviewed and signed by the Municipal Accountant before processing for
payment to ensure the completeness, propriety, and compliance with
accounting rules and regulations; and (iii) coordinate with the Municipal
Accountant with regard to submission of necessary documents to support
the expenditures noted with deficiencies.
c. Municipal Accountant always check the completeness and veracity of
supporting documents prior to processing of claims in compliance with
Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Treasurer and other Accountable Officers to refrain from processing cash
advances for procurements. Also, ensure that all purchases by the Municipality
pass through appropriate procurement procedures as prescribed under the
Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184, and that all required supporting documents are
duly completed and submitted to establish the legality and propriety of the
transactions.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the (i) Municipal Accountant,
in coordination with the Human Resource Management Office (HRMO) identify
all employees without GSIS membership accounts from whom salary deductions
have been made and undertake the necessary procedures for the establishment of
their GSIS membership accounts; and (ii) Municipal Treasurer to promptly remit
the withheld contributions once membership has been established.
For the unaccounted balances under Due to Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth
accounts, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal
Treasurer jointly conduct a comprehensive reconciliation to determine which
employees’ remittances remain unremitted. Further, they should also maintain
complete records for these accounts and put in place proper monitoring and
control measures to ensure that all statutory deductions are remitted accurately
and on time.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, thru the HRMO and in coordination
with all the departments, create a committee that would formulate the required
staffing plan and standard policy guidelines or procedure on the hiring of
personnel under Job orders, Contractual Personnel and Consultants, to
ascertain the propriety, necessity and regularity thereof.
We further recommended that the HRMO (i) strictly comply with the
provisions of COA-DBM JC No. 2, Series of 2020, and COA Circular No. 2009-
001, particularly regarding the timely submission of JO contracts and supporting
documents to the Audit Team for proper review and evaluation; (ii) review and
address the discrepancies noted between the HRMO’s JO list and the gratuity
payroll to ensure compliance with Budget Circular No. 2024-8; and (iii) establish
a centralized and regularly updated JO database, implement regular
reconciliation with payroll records, and strengthen internal controls to promote
accuracy, accountability, and adherence to applicable laws and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor along with the other
municipal officials (i) provide sufficient legal basis to substantiate the
reasonableness of the communication allowance rates applied; (ii) otherwise,
adopt the prescribed rates under DBM BC No. 2024-2.
Also, as stated in our written reply to the query raised by the Municipal
Treasurer, we strongly encourage the Municipality to seek formal clarification
directly from the DBM, as provided in Section 11 of the aforementioned budget
circular, to ensure consistent application and compliance with the prescribed
guidelines.

observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant issued demand letters but only once during CY 2024, dated March 18, 2024. However, they reported that some current employees have agreed to settle their unliquidated cash advances through salary deductions. The Municipal Accountant also issued demand letters on March 18, 2024, to some resigned/transferred employees. The Accounting Office is still in the process of identifying the complete addresses of the remaining individuals. The LGU is currently completing the documentary requirements for the request for write-off. Cash advances were still granted to officials and employees with existing unliquidated cash advances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to confirm the existence of the biological assets and thereafter prepare an adjusting entry, if any, to derecognize the recorded balance of the biological assets and any affected accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office already derecognized the recorded balance of the biological assets since the said assets/breeding stocks were already dispersed to the beneficiaries and no longer owned by the municipality.

observations.recommendation: We commended Management for consistent adherence to Section 287 of R.A. 7160 by providing the required amount of IRA for development projects, as well as compliance to DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1, s. 2020 by including only eligible projects, all for the benefits of its constituents.
observations.managementAction: Management is committed to continuing compliance with Section 287 of R.A. 7160 and DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1, s. 2020.

observations.recommendation:
We encouraged Management to continue their efforts in implementing their prioritized PPAs under the 20% Development Fund for the benefits of its constituents.

However, we also recommended that Management ensure that PPAs funded out of the 20% DF are implemented within the period specified in the AIP. For PPAs that were still unimplemented positive efforts should be made to fast track the completion of the said PPAs.
observations.managementAction: The Management committed to continue their efforts in implementing their prioritized PPAs under the 20% Development Fund.
observations.reason: Of the ten (10) PPAs not implemented in CY 2023, three (3) remained for implementation and are ongoing in CY 2024. Management reported that one (1) of these was implemented/ completed in the first quarter of CY 2025, another has been reprogrammed, and the remaining project is currently under consideration for reprogramming.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management requires the Special Disbursing Officer for Confidential Fund to ensure that Disbursement Voucher for the grant of cash advance for CF have complete supporting documents; and

Disbursement vouchers with covering transmittal letter enumerating the supporting documents, in a sealed envelope, should be submitted within seven (7) days after the release of check to COA ATL for post audit.
observations.managementAction:
Management is taking measures to ensure that the granting of cash advances for Confidential Funds is supported by complete and proper documentation.

The Disbursement Vouchers (DV), along with their supporting documents, were submitted to the audit team in a timely manner.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management stop the payment of gasolines through cash advances of identified department heads moreover cash advances of elective officials; and

The Municipality should consider entering into a contract from identified direct supplier of Petroleum, Oil and Lubricants (POL) In compliance with Annex H of Revised IRR of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction: Management has made the necessary efforts to comply with the audit recommendations. Moreover, fuel purchases are now made directly through an identified and authorized supplier.

observations.recommendation:
We encouraged Management to continue their efforts in implementing their prioritized PPAs under the 20% Development Fund for the benefits of its constituents. However, we also recommended that management ensure that PPAs funded out of the 20% DF are implemented within the period specified in the AIP.

For PPAs that were still unimplemented positive efforts should be made to fast track the completion of the said PPAs.

There are 10 projects for CY 2023 that were still unimplemented as of December
31, 2023.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

Locate all available documents regarding the abovementioned accounts in order to prepare the necessary subsidiary ledgers; and

Exhaust all possible remedies to locate records or documents of the balances of long outstanding receivables in order to require the concerned agencies/organizations to immediately cause the liquidation and settlement of the same.

Also, we reiterated our recommendation that after exhaustion of all the possible remedies to exact liquidation, an assessment be made on the collectability of the receivables and if write-off is warranted, request for the write-off of dormant the accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant adjust/correct the balances of the affected accounts to effect the non-accrual in the agency’s financial statements.

Perform constant review of transactions to deter error and omissions.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management stop the practice of granting cash advances to employees for expenditures/disbursements that can be paid directly through checks. In case there is an activity of the agency undertaken in the field and it is impractical to pay the same by check the Municipal Mayor should designate a Special Disbursing Officer.

Further, we recommended that only cash advances for travelling be granted to elective officials.
observations.managementAction: The noted transactions were already directly paid through checks. Special Disbursing officer were designated.
observations.reason: The LGU still granted cash advances other than travel to elected officials.

observations.recommendation:
We commended management for their effort in trying to disclose the balance of the Trust Liability – DRRM account in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, however, we reiterated our recommendation that the amounts and details should be properly presented/disclosed.

Also, we recommended that management ensure that trust obligations have sufficient cash back-up.
observations.managementAction: The Trust Liability – DRRM account is properly disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statement in compliance to COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
observations.reason: the Trust Liabilities – DRRMF balance per books of was not sufficiently funded.

observations.recommendation:
We commended management for registering a positive collection efficiency rate for Business Tax and Fees and Charges during the year 2022.

However, since Real Property Tax posted a deficiency collection rate of 17.69% during the current year, we recommended that management direct the concerned officials and employees to enforce a more vigorous and effective tax campaign/collection strategy to attain 100% accomplishment or more in the collection of its targeted income for the year, so that more programs, projects and activities can be implemented in the future, and to minimize the risk of revenue delinquencies.
observations.reason: The LGU still did not reach the estimates/ targets set by the BLGF.

observations.recommendation: Based on the foregoing, we recommended that management conduct more extensive tax campaign so that landowners are apprised of the advantages/services that the Municipality would render if taxes are paid. Develop an efficient and effective monitoring and collection system so as to intensify the collection of its outstanding RPT/SET Receivables. Moreover, avail of all the possible remedies provided under the Rules and Regulations Implementing the Local Government Code of 1991 to enforce collection of RPT.
observations.reason: Large delinquencies still exist.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipality revisit the reasonableness of the amount of communication allowance granted to their officials and employees. The Municipality should consider the cheaper prevailing rates offered by mobile networks to decrease the amount of the communication allowance to a more conscionable rate.

Further, we also recommended that claims for communication allowance should be properly supported with documents such as Official receipts for the purchase of cell cards or payments of mobile plan, the frequency of official calls, the name/office of persons being contacted by them and other necessary documents.
observations.managementAction: A Sanggunian Bayan Ordinance was passed to reduce the communication allowance received by the Municipal Officials.
observations.reason: Disbursements for communication allowance were still not supported with necessary documents.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that Management:
Prioritize the reconciliation of balances between the accounting records and RPCPPE for fair presentation of PPE accounts in the Financial Statements; and
After reconciliation, Management should consider disposing the unserviceable, request from COA for the derecognition of non-existing and missing PPEs from the Agency’s book of accounts, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, otherwise known as the Guidelines and Procedures in the Conduct of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), Recognition of PPE Items Found at Station, and Disposition for Non-existing/Missing PPE Items, for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances of Government Agencies.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and take up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are adjusted on timely basis; and
Perform constant review of transactions to deter error and omissions.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to confirm the existence of the biological assets and thereafter prepare an adjusting entry, if any, to derecognize the recorded balance of the biological assets and any affected accounts.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to identify the cause or reason for the deficit or cash overdraft. Thereafter, the Municipal Accountant should carry out the necessary actions to ensure that Trust Liabilities-DRMMF have a sufficient cash back-up.

Based on the foregoing, we reiterated our recommendations that Management:
Conduct more extensive tax campaign so that landowners are apprised of the advantages/services that the Municipality would render if taxes are paid;
b.  Develop an efficient and effective monitoring and collection system so as to intensify the collection of its outstanding RPT/SET Receivables; and
c. Avail of all the possible remedies provided under the Rules and Regulations Implementing the Local Government Code of 1991 to enforce the collection of RPT.

We encouraged Management to continue their efforts in implementing their prioritized PPAs under the 20% Development Fund for the benefits of its constituents.
However, we also recommended that Management ensure that PPAs funded out of the 20% DF are implemented within the period specified in the AIP. For PPAs that were still unimplemented positive efforts should be made to fast track the completion of the said PPAs.

We recommended that Management ensure that all budgeted projects, programs and activities are optimally and fully implemented to timely bring the desired benefits to the intended users. For PPAs that were still unimplemented positive efforts should be made to fast track the completion of the said PPAs.

We recommended that:
Management requires the Special Disbursing Officer for Confidential Fund to ensure that Disbursement Voucher for the grant of cash advance for CF have complete supporting documents; and
Disbursement vouchers with covering transmittal letter enumerating the supporting documents, in a sealed envelope, should be submitted within seven (7) days after the release of check to COA ATL for post audit

We recommended that Management instruct
Stop the payment of gasolines through cash advances of identified department heads moreover cash advances of elective officials; and
The Municipality should consider entering into a contract from identified direct supplier of Petroleum, Oil and Lubricants (POL) In compliance with Annex H of Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Treasurer should strictly require the concerned collecting officer to remit his collections daily to avoid duplication of his unremitted collections. Also, monitor the issuance of accountable forms, make sure that he was only given new accountable forms after surrendering the used accountable forms.

Henceforth, the OIC-Municipal Treasurer observes the above-mentioned provision of DOF-BLGF Local Treasury Operations Manual (LTOM) to protect the funds of the municipality. Collections of all Collecting Officer should be diligently turned over to the OIC-Municipal Treasurer/ Liquidating officer daily for the Collecting Officers assigned at the Office and at least once a week or as soon as the collections reach P5,000.00 for field collectors.
observations.managementAction:
Collections were remitted within the day and issuance of accountable forms were monitored.








The OIC-Municipal Treasurer is strictly requiring the Collection Officer to turn-over their collections to the Liquidating Officers within the prescribed period.

ensure that the minimum budgetary requirement for GAD Fund provided in JMC No. 2013-01 is satisfied.  In doing this, he should properly classify the PPAs as to mandated or attributed programs.   Likewise, he should see to it that the mandated programs identified in the GPB are provided with separate appropriations in the budgets.  He should also coordinate with other offices in conducting gender analysis through the administration of HGDG on their respective programs to be able to determine if portion of their budgets may be attributed to GAD; 

carefully prepare the GAD AR with full disclosure of the necessary information for the benefit of the users thereof; and

see to it that the GAD AR is supported with the actions taken by the LGU on the COA audit findings and recom-mendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, being the HoA, be properly guided by the provisions of the JC No. 2015-01 to be able to act accordingly in the budgeting, release, utilization and reporting of the CF.  We further recommended that he facilitate the submission of the Quarterly Accomplishment Report to the DILG Secretary in compliance with the JC.  The same should be submitted to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.
observations.reason: Analysis of the Annual Budget for CY 2021 showed that no allocations were provided for CF.   An        amount of P200,000.00 was appropriated for the LGU’s Peace and Order Programs (POP).   As stated in Item 5.1.3.1 of the JC, the total amount for CF shall not exceed thirty percent (30%) of the total annual amount allocated for the LGU’s POP.  The determination as to compliance with the said requirement could not be made since the allocation of CF was not provided.   The required Physical and Financial Plan was likewise not attached in the Annual Budget.  Item 4.6 of the JC also provides that the release and utilization of the total CF for LGUs shall be covered by a resolution duly approved by two-thirds (2/3) of the total membership of the Local Peace and Order Council.  The same was not submitted to the Audit Team.

This will serve as a tool in informing management on the condition as well as the status of operations of the special account.
observations.reason: The Municipality still did not maintain special account for 20% DF

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to evaluate and correctly classify PPE items into their proper account classification in accordance with the revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Coordinate and reconcile the records with Municipal Assessors Office and Inventory and Appraisal Committee to ascertain the correctness of the Land account.
Prepare Journal Entry Vouchers to record the cost/value assigned by the Inventory and Appraisal Committee based on the market/fair value for properties found at the station.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to correct the misclassification of accounts with the following suggested Pro-forma adjusting journal entry in the General Fund books of accounts, as follows:
Account			   Debit			Credit
Other Payables				 xxx
Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable						xxx

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to validate the credit memo reflected in bank statements and draw journal entry vouchers to record the reconciling items that require adjustments, thereby, ensuring the fair presentation of the cash account in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Analyze the Loans Payable and its related interest to ensure proper recognition of the current and non-current portion; and
Prepare an adjusting entry to rectify the errors in recording the interest and principal portion of payments committed in prior years.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to provide adequate disclosures on the Loans Payable account so that the liquidity and solvency of the Municipality could be properly assessed.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the accrued expenses, i.e accrued interest, are recorded in accordance with accrual basis and International Public Sector Accounting Standards, moving forward.
We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain a special account in the General Fund for all loans availed by the Municipality.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Update themselves on the rules and regulations pertaining to the warranty security required in the procurement of goods; and
Ensure that for future procurements, retain an amount equivalent to at least 1% of the contract price or require a special bank guarantee of the same amount from the suppliers,  to guarantee that that supplier will be liable for any manufacturing defects.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the Perpetual Inventory Method of accounting by taking up first the construction materials procured, under the Construction Materials Inventory, upon purchase. Also, the Municipal Accountant must be meticulous in recording future purchases of medical supplies to prevent recurring errors.

We recommended that Management ensure transparency in government procurement and:
Require the BAC Secretariat to
12.10.1 Update themselves on the rules and regulations on the posting requirement of the Notices of Award and Notices to Proceed, to ensure the on-time posting of the same to the PhilGEPS website, the Municipality’s official website, and the three conspicuous places;
12.10.2	Attach a Certification of the posting of Notice of Award in the conspicuous places, upon submission of the contract documents; and
12.10.3	Direct the Municipal Administrator to fast track the updating of the Municipality’s official website, and immediately post the Notices of Award and Notices to Proceed, thereto.

We recommended that Management, through its Inventory Committee, conduct complete physical count of the properties of the Municipality, reconcile the results of the count with the accounting records and submit the complete and accurate RPCI and RPCPPE to the Audit Team, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant observe the proper account classification of advance payments of taxes by the taxpayers and henceforth prepare the adjusting entries by (i) debiting the Real Property Tax Receivable and crediting Other Prepayments accounts in the amount of P223,119.77 in the General Fund Books and (ii) debiting Special Education Tax Receivable and crediting Other Prepayments in the amount of P278,899.72 in the Special Education Fund Books.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the:
Municipal Treasurer (i) carefully evaluate the disbursement vouchers submitted to him to be able to issue the appropriate checks; and (ii) effect a fund transfer amounting to P441,345.00 for the disbursements of various COVID-19 PPAs that should have been paid out of the Calamity Fund bank account but were erroneously charged against the Trust Fund - Proper bank account;
BAC (i) see to it that POs and their supporting documents, irrespective of amount, are submitted to the Audit Team within the period prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-001; (ii) properly accomplish the POs by providing complete information such as the date of delivery, delivery term and the specific mode of procurement; and (iii) ensure that consistent information are presented in the related documents; and
Municipal Accountant (i) record an entry debiting the Due to Other Funds and crediting the Trust Liabilities - DRRMF account for the fund transfer of the 20 percent DF to the Calamity Fund bank account; (ii) record an adjusting entry for the disbursements totaling P406,620.00 which should have been debited to Trust Liabilities - DRRMF, instead of Due to Other Funds account.  Both entries should be recorded in the Trust Fund.; (iii) ensure that all the sources of fund and the corresponding disbursements of COVID-19 PPAs are accounted in the List of Vouchers and Annex A - Summary of COVID-19 Fund Sources and Utilization in order to monitor the implementation thereof; and (iv) disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements and the Report on Utilization of DRRMF the transactions affecting the Calamity Fund bank account.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the:
BAC (i) strictly comply with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 in the procurement process undertaken for the projects of the Municipality and (ii) carefully prepare the documents/reports in order to provide complete and accurate  information to the users thereof; and
Municipal Engineer, who acts as the BAC Vice-Chairperson and the BAC-TWG Head, see to it that the guidelines provided in the DPWH DO No. 197 series of 2016 are considered during the preparation of ABC.  In addition to the ABC, he should also prepare the documents such as POW and DEE with utmost care towards achieving accuracy of the information indicated therein.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the:
Municipal Accountant (i) properly account the collections derived from the municipal situs tax and taxes on sand and gravel imposed on the contractor in accordance with the provisions of Section 138 of R.A. No. 7160 in order to accurately present the balances of the affected liability and income accounts at year-end; (ii) see to it that all the documentary requirements enumerated in COA Circular No. 2012-001 were fully complied with; and (iii) properly evaluate the information contained in the documents attached to the claims; and
Municipal Engineer (i) thoroughly check the information contained in the SWA, Statement of Time Elapsed and Work Accomplished and Overall Progress Bar Chart and S-Curve to be able to present accurate and reliable information to the users thereof.  He should also pay particular attention on the value of previous accomplishments forwarded in the succeeding SWA in the case of progress payments.  Careful review of the SWA is necessary in evaluating the total value of accomplishments the contractors are entitled to and the corresponding percentages of completion of the projects.; and (ii) communicate with the Head of the Procuring Entity the right-of-way problems encountered during the preparation of plans/drawings of the project for immediate action thereof.  With this, the contractor will smoothly perform the works in accordance with the schedule set by the Procuring Entity.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the BAC:
ensure strict compliance with the requirements prescribed under the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to prevent the same lapses in future procurements;
see to it that the information contained in the documents/reports being prepared are consistent; and
ensure the timely submission of the perfected contracts and the documents forming part thereof to the Audit Team in compliance with Items 3.1.1 and 3.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the:
Municipal Mayor and other concerned accountable officers ensure that the guidelines on the grant of the CNA Incentive for CY 2021 provided in DBM Budget Circular No. 2021-3 are strictly observed; and
Municipal Accountant (i) submit a report showing in detail the PPAs which were accomplished as of September 30, 2021; and (ii) record the payment of CNA Incentive to Other Personnel Benefits account in compliance with the Circular.

observations.recommendation: However, the remaining two (2) unimplemented projects which were available for disbursement for the year should be implemented without further delay.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant perform a detailed reconciliation of records of the cash accounts and draw adjusting/correcting entries as necessary.

We also reiterated our recommendation that:
The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer should periodically reconcile their records; and
The Municipality maintain an updated and accurate subsidiary records for each bank accounts. Thereafter, posting of transactions affecting the cash accounts should be made real time.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management explain and/or justify the incurrence of cash deficit/overdraft in the DRRM Fund.

Also, we recommended that a detailed reconcilaition be made by the Accounting and Treasury Office such that adjusting entries be drawn, as necessary in the Trust Liability – DRRM account.

Lastly, we recommended that management ensure that trust liability obligations have sufficient cash back-up.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the balances of the affected accounts in order to fairly present the amounts in the agency’s financial statements.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Locate all available documents regarding the abovementioned accounts in order to prepare the necessary subsidiary ledgers; and
Exhaust all possible remedies to locate records or documents of the balances of long outstanding receivables in order to require the concerned agencies/organizations to immediately cause the liquidation and settlement of the same.
Also, we recommended that after exhaustion of the all the possible remedies to exact liquidation, an assessment be made on the collectability of the receivables and if write-off is warranted, request for the write-off of dormant the accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant adjust/correct the balances of the affected accounts to effect the non-accrual in the agency’s financial statements.

Also, we reiterated our recommendations that Management:
Direct the Municipal Assessor to prepare and submit to the Municipal Treasurer, on or before the thirty-first (31st) day of December each year, an assessment roll containing a list of all persons whose real properties have been newly assessed or reassessed and the values of such properties.
Require the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant of a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year at the beginning of the year as basis to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the debit to Real Property Tax Receivable/Special Education Tax Receivable and crediting to Deferred Real Property Tax Income/Deferred Special Education Tax Income.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accounting Office record all purchases to its corresponding inventory account classification and to adjust the account for whatever consumption on the basis of Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI), duly supported with the original copy of Requisition Issue Slip (RIS).

Also, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and General Services Officer maintain Supplies Ledger Cards (SLC) for each commodity/stock.

Lastly, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive require periodic physical inventory of supplies to be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively, in accordance to the provisions of Section 124 of LGU NGAS Volume I.

We recommended that management instruct the:
The Municipal Accountant to derecognized the PPE accounts and depreciation expenses for the semi-expendable assets and repairs and maintenance and to recognize the corresponding Inventory and/or expense account for proper presetation in Financial Statements.
The General Service Officer to cancel the AREs issued to end user and changed it to Inventory Custodian Sip (ICS).

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Accountant:
Trace the history of the account;
After tracing, determine the extent of completion of the project/s; and
Draw adjusting and/or correcting entries as necessary.

We recommended that management require the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to maintain a detailed Equipment Ledger Card for each category of Property, Plant and Equipment.

Also, we reiterated our recommendations that Management:
Prioritize the reconciliation of balances between the accounting records and RPCPPE. Prepare adjusting entries, if any, to reflect the correct balance of the affected accounts; and
After reconciliation, management should consider disposing the unserviceable, 
non-existing and missing PPEs, and derecognize such from the Agency’s book of accounts, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended that management require the Municipal Accountant to trace the expenditures for road networks in CYs 2016-2018 that were recorded in various PPE accounts and to reclassify thereof in the Road Networks account.

Also, we reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant, GSO and Municipal Engineer to exert efforts in locating documents and information pertaining to the road networks of the LGU for CY 2015 and prior years to properly recognize the same in the LGU’s Financial Statements for a fair presentation of the PPE account.
Direct the above-mentioned key officials to accomplish the duties and responsibilities assigned to them; and
Ensure compliance with accounting and reporting guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015.

We recommended that management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to follow the prescribed useful life in the computation of depreciation; and
Recompute the depreciation in CYs 2021 and prior years, after recomputation, adjust the books accordingly.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Accountant:
Trace the history of the account;
After tracing, determine the extent of completion of the project/s; and
Draw adjusting and/or correcting entries as necessary.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Locate all available documents regarding the abovementioned accounts in order to prepare the necessary subsidiary ledgers; and
Exhaust all possible remedies to reconcile the balances of those payable accounts in which SL is maintained. Prepare necessary adjusting entries, if any.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Locate all available documents regarding the abovementioned accounts in order to prepare the necessary subsidiary ledgers; and
After locating the documents pertaining to the above indicated balances of long outstanding payables and unremitted taxes withheld, immediately cause the liquidation and/or settlement of the same. If the amounts do not represent valid obligations of the agency, correcting/adjusting entries should be made.

We recommended that management:
direct the Municipal Accountant to be vigilant in charging the proper account whenever a fund is received from outside sources; and
draw adjusting journal entries to correct the misstatements noted in the affected accounts.
we recommended that the amounts and details should be properly presented/disclosed and sufficiently substantiated.

We recommended that management:
Explain/justify the appropriateness of charging the purchase of air-conditioning equipment against the unexpended balance of Trust Liability-DRRMF; and
Draw adjusting journal entries to recognize the purchased air-conditioning equipment in the General Fund books.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Revisit the entry made on Due from Other Funds in CY 2017 and explain why no counterpart entry on Due to Other Funds was made;
Prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and take up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are adjusted on timely basis; and
Perform constant review of transactions to deter error and omissions.

We recommended that management:
Update and post notice of delinquency for property taxes at the main entrance of the municipal building and in a publicly accessible and conspicuous place in each barangay where these properties were located.
Seek authority and assistance from the provincial government as well as the concerned barangays for the issuance of Notices of Delinquency and in the service of Warrants of Levy to delinquent real property owners.
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to avail of all the possible remedies provided under the Rules and Regulations Implementing the Local Government Code of 1991 to enforce collection of RPT.
Also, we reiterated our recommendation that the agency conduct more extensive tax campaign so that landowners are apprised of the advantages/services that the Municipality would render if taxes are paid. Develop an efficient and effective monitoring and collection system so as to intensify the collection of its outstanding RPT/SET Receivables.

We recommended that management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to explain/explore the cause of the discrepancy of the records mentioned above and if adjusting/correcting entries is warranted, the same should be drawn; and
Exercise vigilance in recording the transactions of the LGU.

We reiterated our recommendation that the municipal accountant should transfer the total amount of accumulated interest income and business tax from the Trust Fund to the General Fund through the following journal entry:

CY 2021
CY 2020

Any interest that may be earned from Trust Fund deposits during the current and future years should be taken up in the accounts of the year to which the income relates, pending the actual transfer of funds, as follows:

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Accountant:
Explain the presence of incorrectly classified accounts in the agency’s financial statements; and
Exercise vigilance whenever journal entries are made.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant maintain complete subsidiary ledgers for the special accounts in the general fund and prepare the corresponding financial reports to form part of the schedules of the general fund trial balance as required under the abovementioned guidelines.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant:
To draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to immediately recognize the unutilized amount of LDRRMF-QRF and the LDRRMF- MITIGATION FUND MOOE for CY 2021, pending the actual transfer of cash to the Special Trust Fund account.
To ensure that the above entry should always be made annually before the preparation of year-end financial statements.

We recommended that management:
direct the Municipal Accountant to transfer only the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE unexpended/unobligated balances in the Trust Fund books. The LDRRMF for CO is to be made continuing in the General Fund until the projects funded therefore are completed; and
draw adjusting journal entries to correct the misstatement noted as a result of the improper treatment of the unutilized balance of LDRRMF.

We recommended that the Municipality:
Fully implement/utilize the planned programs, activities and projects under the DRRM Fund so that the intended benefits would be fully achieved; and
Revisit the feasibility of its LDRRMIP in order to prevent delay in the implementation of its planned programs/projects/activities.

We recommended the management that the GAD Fund should be utilized for specific and attainable project, programs or services that would address gender issues and women empowerment. Also, utilize the GAD Budget in accordance with the GAD Plan and exercise due care and prudence in disbursing government funds in order to make sure that these are spent properly, and the best value is derived out of it.

We recommended that management utilize the LCPC Fund based on the approved Programs/Projects/Activities to fully achieved its objective of promoting the general welfare of children and protecting their rights including those Child at Risk and Children in Conflict with the Law.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly adhere to the provisions of DBM Local Budget Circular in the Project Implementation  of the Local Government Support Fund – Other Financial Assistance, for transparency and accountability.

we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned officials to submit to the audit team the necessary supporting documents, such as those enumerated above, to prove the entitlement of the officials/employees to the SRA under AO No. 36 as amended.

Submit a brief justification/explanation for each official/ employee as to how said official/ employee has complied with such requirement as PHW as defined under AO No. 36 dated November 16, 2020 and reiterated under DBM – DOH Joint Circular No. 2, s. 2020 dated November 25, 2020. The justification/explanation for each official/employee may be shown in tabular form as to how he/she qualified for the claimed allowance.

we recommended that management:
Explain/justify the transfer of appropriated amount for the Carabao Development Program and refund of cash advances from the General Fund to the Trust Fund. The justification should properly explain the eligibility of recognition of the said amounts in the Trust Fund books. Thereafter, draw adjusting journal entries, as necessary, for the effects presented in the financial statements; and
Always follow relevant laws, rules and regulations in the recognition of agency transactions.

We recommended that Management:
instruct the Municipal Mayor and Vice-Mayor to justify the necessity of having numerous JO employees in their Office/Department despite exceeding their appropriations and the Accomplishment Reports, signed by them, of the JO employees under them showed duplication of works, to wit:
Limit disbursement for the wages of JO employees up to the amount that was actually appropriated;
Ensure that hiring of job order employees are based on necessity of the Office/Department to avoid waste of government funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor submit justification, with complete supporting documents, why the wages of the above-mentioned JO employees totaling to P171,425.00  should not be disallowed in audit.

We commended the Municipality in their efforts to collect the unsettled disallowances from the persons liable. However, we still reccomend that the COE should be enforced by the Municipal Mayor. If settlement cannot be made in full by the persons liable, advise them to file a written Request to the COA for authority to settlle through installment basis considering that the nature of the disallowance,  is allowed to be settled through installment.

We recommended that Management, through its Inventory Committee, conduct complete physical count of properties of the Municipality, reconcile the results of the count with the accounting records and submit the complete and accurate RPCI and RPCPPE to the Audit Team, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the: 
	
MDRRMO (i) see to it that the relevant provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 are strictly adhered to during the preparation of the LDRRMFIP.  He should also coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer regarding the status of the projects classified as CO on previous years in order to present accurate information of continuing projects in the Plan; and (ii) include in the template – Inventory of Equipment Purchased Out of DRRMF/ Donatio ns all the equipment purchased both in the current and prior years.  Also, he should see to it that the cost of these equipment were indicated therein.      

Municipal Accountant (i) effect the following adjusting entries (1) an amount of P24,000.00 pertaining to the financial assistance given to households affected by Typhoon “Tisoy” and (2) an amount of P784,155.76 in the books of the Trust Fund relative to the fund transfer effected in May 2017 from the Trust Fund - Proper Bank Account No. 1162-1044-18 to General Fund Bank Account No. 1162-1018-26; (ii) comply with Items 4.1 and 5.1.12 of the Circular on the accounting of funds received from the Office of the President; and (iii) strictly adhere to Item 5.1.10 of the Circular which states that all unexpended/ unobligated balance of the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE shall be transferred to the Special Trust Fund to be able to present the correct balance of the Trust Liabilities - DRRMF account.  Furthermore, she should properly disclose the amounts and details of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF in the Notes to Financial Statements; and

Municipal Treasurer effect a fund transfer on the following transactions: (i) an amount of P108,000.00 from the Calamity Fund bank account to the Trust Fund - Proper bank account pertaining to the transfer of unutilized balance of DRRMF for CY 2011 to the General Fund erroneously charged against the latter account; (ii) an amount of P45,750.00 from the Calamity Fund bank account to the General Fund bank account pertaining to the purchase of materials for the Rehabilitation of Busay Falls Eco-Tourism Park which was funded from the 70% Mitigation Fund for CY 2015 maintained in the Trust Fund.   This was initially charged against the Trust Fund which the Audit Team found proper.  However, it was later on paid by the General Fund; and (iii) an amount of P64,912.00 from the Calamity Fund bank account to the General Fund bank account for the overstatement of the unexpended balance of 70% Mitigation Fund – MOOE for CY 2017 that was transferred to the Special Trust Fund in CY 2018.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the: 

Procuring Entity strictly comply with Section 17.6 of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9814 to avoid incurring delay in the imple-mentation of projects; 





Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant (i) jointly check whether the documentary requirements enumerated in COA Circular No. 2012-001 were fully complied with; (ii) see to it that the amount due to the contractor to be reflected in the disbursement voucher is reconciled with the total value of accomplish-ments reported in the SWA; and (iii) ensure that the amount of the warranty security posted by the contractor adhere to the guidelines set forth in Section 62.2.3.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184; and
 
Municipal Engineer (i) inform the contractors that it is their responsibility to prepare and submit the documents such as SWA, Statement of Time Elapsed and Work Accomplished and Overall Progress Bar Chart and S-Curve; and (ii) thoroughly check the information contained in the SWA, Statement of Time Elapsed and Work Accomplished and Overall Progress Bar Chart and S-Curve to be able to present accurate and reliable information to the users thereof.  He should also pay particular attention on the value of previous accomplish-ments forwarded in the succeeding SWA in the case of progress payments.   Careful review of the SWA is necessary in evaluating the total value of accomplish-ments the contractors are entitled to and the corresponding percentages of completion of the projects.
observations.managementAction:
Audit of the payments of infrastructure projects revealed that the Procuring Entity encountered right-of-way problem on the project – Upgrading of Barangay Cagboaya Local Access Road.  The Municipal Engineer explained in his reply that coordinations with the electric cooperative were made before the project imple-mentation.  However, the electric cooperative became busy in repairing the damages brought about by Typhoon “Rolly” and “Ulysses”.  This prompted them to allow the contractor to commence the actual construction pending the resolution of the right-of-way problem.  He further stated that had they waited for the problem to be solved before the actual construction, the project could have been completed later than March 26, 2021.  

The Audit Team observed that the Contractor’s Affidavit was already attached in the progress payments.  Furthermore, the warranty security posted by the contractors was found sufficient.  The amount due the contractor indicated in the voucher is also reconciled with the total value of accomplish-ments reported in the SWA.      




Verification showed that the documents such as SWA, Statement of Time Elapsed and Work Accomplished and Overall Progress Bar Chart and S-Curve were already prepared by the contractor.
observations.reason: Deficiencies were still noted in the SWA, Statement of Time Elapsed and Work Accomplished and Overall Progress Bar Chart and S-Curve attached in the disbursement vouchers.

Verification of the Statement of Financial Performance for CY 2021 of the Municipality showed that the Share from the IRA still constitutes a greater portion of its total revenues equivalent to 89.92 percent. observations.recommendation: We recommended the management to appropriate the LCPC funds to projects or programs that is more responsive to the needs of the children, specially those children at risk and children in conflict with the law, rather than appropriating the majority of LCPC fund to payment of honoraria. Also, always act for the best interest of the children as the LCPC fund was created.

We recommended that the LDRRMO undertake the following actions: (a) ensure that the current appropriations incorporated in the LDRRMFIP were reconciled with the appropriations provided in the budgets in order to arrive at the correct amount of unexpended balances of LDRRMF; (b) keep track of the projects with continuing appropriations since these should be considered in the preparation of the Plan.  Proper monitoring of these projects will also serve as a control measure in the disbursements to be charged therefrom; (c) stopped the practice of providing lump-sum appropriations using the generic term “Capital Outlay” in the Plan since only the allocation for 30% QRF shall be presented in lump-sum appropriations; (d) submit to the Audit Team the supplemental LDRRMFIP for the projects funded from the unexpended balances of 30% QRF for CYs 2014 to 2018 and the savings from the 70% Mitigation Fund – MOOE for CY 2019 if indeed it was prepared before disbursements were made; (e) properly classify the projects to be charged against the Calamity Fund to arrive at the correct amounts of unexpended balances of 70% Mitigation Fund – MOOE and CO; and (f) include all the equipment purchased out of DRRMF (both current year and prior years) in the template – Inventory of Equipment Purchased Out of DRRMF/
Donations as of December 31, 2019.

We further recommended that the LDRRMO provide the Audit Team on the source of fund used in the disbursements totaling P78,297.00 for the Fabrication of Collapsible Tents.  This is to be undertaken to be able to present the correct amounts of unexpended balance of 70% Mitigation Fund – MOOE for CYs 2015 and 2016.  The Municipal Accountant and the LDRRMO shall also coordinate during the preparation of the related documents/reports towards achieving reconciliation of the amounts and consistency of information.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) transfer back to the Trust Fund the unexpended balances of 30% QRF for CYs 2017 and 2018 amounting to P182,822.40 and P1,418,762.63, respectively, since these are still available for use in disaster risk reduction and management activities on the succeeding years until such time that the five-year period already lapsed.  Relative to this, a fund transfer amounting to P5,996,303.03 shall be made from the Calamity Fund bank account to the Trust Fund – Proper bank account; (b) ensure that the service provider had already performed its services before payments were made thereto; (c) cause a fund transfer amounting to P108,000.00 from the Calamity Fund bank account to the Trust Fund – Proper bank account pertaining to the unutilized balance of DRRMF for CY 2011 reverted to the General Fund which was taken from the latter account; (d) account the unexpended balance of 30% QRF and 70% Mitigation Fund – MOOE for CY 2019 and immediately transfer to the Special Trust Fund in compliance with Item 5.1.10 of the Circular; (e) see to it that all the disbursements pertaining to the projects charged against the 5% Calamity Fund were accounted in the List of Vouchers/Projects for 5% Calamity Fund, Report on Utilization of DRRMF and the disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements to achieve correct amount of unexpended balances of LDRRMF; (f) make a fund transfer amounting to P45,750.00 from Trust Fund to General Fund pertaining to the purchase of materials for the project – Rehabilitation of Busay Falls Eco-Tourism Park which should be charged against the former fund; and (g) carefully prepare the disclosure on the amounts and details of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF in the Notes to Financial Statements to provide the users thereof with complete and accurate information.   

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer along with other members of the BAC properly plan the procurement schedule on the projects implemented by administration to prevent the situations wherein the projects were already completed even before the delivery of the construction materials.  The Municipal Engineer is also advised to commence the actual construction of the projects once the needed materials were already delivered by the winning suppliers.

The MDRRMO also provided the source of fund used in the disbursements totaling P78,297.00 for the Fabrication of Collapsible Tents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, being the HoA, be properly guided by the provisions of the JC No. 2015-01 to be able to act accordingly in the budgeting, release, utilization and reporting of the CF.  We further recommended that he facilitate the submission of the Quarterly Accomplishment Report to the DILG Secretary in compliance with the JC.  The same should be submitted to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.
observations.reason: Analysis of the Annual Budget for CY 2020 showed that no allocations were provided for CF.  An        amount of P200,000.00 was appropriated for the LGU’s Peace and Order Programs (POP).  As stated in Item 5.1.3.1 of the JC, the total amount for CF shall not exceed thirty percent (30%) of the total annual amount allocated for the LGU’s POP.  The determination as to compliance with the said requirement could not be made since the allocation of CF was not provided.  The required Physical and Financial Plan was likewise not attached in the Annual Budget.  Item 4.6 of the JC also provides that the release and utilization of the total CF for LGUs shall be covered by a resolution duly approved by two-thirds (2/3) of the total membership of the Local Peace and Order Council.  The same was not submitted to the Audit Team.  Verification of the disbursements showed that the reimbursement amounting to P40,000.00 representing the expenses incurred on the  operation against illegal drugs was charged against the appropriations for Peace and Order Council in December 2020.  The said transaction was recorded as debit to Confidential Expenses account.  On the disbursement made, the Audit Team observed that the proper procedures in the release of the CF provided in Item 6.0 of the JC were not followed by the Municipality.  The required report on the use of CF was likewise not submitted to the Audit Team.
