observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that:
The agency should reflect in the agency’s books the valid reconciling items noted in the Bank Reconciliation Statements.
The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer should periodically reconcile their records.
The Municipality maintain an updated and accurate subsidiary records for each bank accounts. Thereafter, posting of transactions affecting the cash accounts should be made real time.
Also, we recommended that the agency perform a General and Subsidiary Ledger reconciliation of the cash accounts and draw adjusting/correcting entries as necessary.
The Management commented that the discrepancies noted were all carried forward from previous years (management) which we currently are tracing. We have tried to check the available vouchers and documents but we were not able to trace the discrepancies.
The Municipality incurred a cash deficit/overdraft in the agency’s Trust Liability -Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund amounting to P3,085,648.29, contrary to Section 337 of RA 7160 and the principles of sound internal control.
Section 158 of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual, Volume I specifically mentioned the provisions of Section 47 of PD 1177 and Section 41, Book VI of the 1987 Administrative Code of the Philippines which prohibits the incurrence of overdrafts (in appropriation or cash).
In the local government units, Section 337 of RA 7160, otherwise known as the Local Government Code of the Philippines provides that:
“No cash overdraft in any local fund shall be incurred at the end of the fiscal year.”
Examination of accounting records and bank statements revealed the following:
As shown in the table above, the accumulated balance of Trust Liability – DRRM as at December 2020 amounting to P9,651,039.39 cannot be accommodated by the cash balance in the Trust-DRRM bank account amounting to P6,511,172.67. This means that when management opts to fully utilize the unexpended balance for its disaster related programs/projects/ activities, a cash overdraft of P3,139,866.72 will occur.
When asked why such scenario happened, the Accounting Department reasoned out incorrect codings and/or charging of expenditures in the Trust Liability - DRRM account.
The Trust Liability – DRRM account is a very straightforward account such that the only movement of the same is the transfer of unexpended balance thereof from the general fund and its subsequent utilization, if there are any. Therefore, sound management dictates that the balance of the account should always equal to the cash balance as reflected in the bank statement.
The incurrence of overdraft is not only violative of the afore-cited law but also reflects inefficient management of cash and poor recordkeeping.
We recommended that Management explain and/or justify the incurrence of cash deficit in the DRRM Fund and to revisit the entries made and effect adjusting entries as necessary in the Trust Liability – DRRM account.
Also, we recommended that management refrain from using trust fund money for purposes other than those for which the trust was created or the funds received pursuant to Section 4 of PD 1445.
Lastly, we recommended that management ensure that trust liability obligations have sufficient cash back-up.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant adjust and/or correct the balances of the affected accounts, through journal entries, in order to properly present the amounts in the financial statements.
The Management commented that the Accounting Department will make the necessary corrections and adjustments.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Locate all available documents regarding the abovementioned accounts in order to prepare the necessary subsidiary ledgers.
Exhaust all possible remedies to locate records or documents of the balances of long outstanding receivables in order to require the concerned agencies/organizations to immediately cause the liquidation and settlement of the same and/or request for the write-off of dormant accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
The Management commented that the audit observation is accepted and admittedly one of the Accounting Department’s burden. Documentation of these receivables can no longer be traced despite all efforts, thus subsidiary ledgers cannot be fully updated and corrected. Numerous demand letters were sent but most did not respond, others actually rejected the receipt of the letter.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant adjust/correct the balances of the affected accounts after determining the net effect of the non-accrual in the agency’s financial statements.
Also, we recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Assessor to prepare and submit to the Municipal Treasurer, on or before the thirty-first (31st) day of December each year, an assessment roll containing a list of all persons whose real properties have been newly assessed or reassessed and the values of such properties.
Require the Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant of a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year at the beginning of the year as basis to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the debit to Real Property Tax Receivable/Special Education Tax Receivable and crediting to Deferred Real Property Tax Income/Deferred Special Education Tax Income.
The Management commented that the Accounting Department together with the Assessor’s Office and Treasurer’s Office will monitor the accrual of Real Property Taxes in the book of accounts.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accounting Office record all purchases to its corresponding inventory account classification and to adjust the account for whatever consumption on the basis of Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI), duly supported with the original copy of Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS), which should be prepared and submitted weekly or monthly to the Municipal Accountant.
The Management committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Trace the history of the account;
After tracing, determine the extent of completion of the project/s; and
Draw adjusting and/or correcting entries as necessary.
The Management commented that the Advances to Contractors are carried forward and there is difficulty of tracing the history of the account due to unavailability of supporting documents.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management:
Prioritize the reconciliation of balances between the accounting records and RPCPPE. Prepare adjusting entries, if any, to reflect the correct balance of the affected accounts.
Should consider disposing the unserviceable PPE, after reconciliation was made, and derecognize such from the Agency’s book of accounts.
Maintain a detailed Equipment Ledger Card for each class of Property, Plant and Equipment that supports the balances presented in the FS. The schedule should enable the user to trace for the acquisition date and cost of an individual PPE to facilitate depreciation computation.
The Management commented that the reasons for un-reconciled difference are some PPEs were not yet booked-up by the Accounting Department which the GSO Office has included in the inventory of all PPEs actually on hand or seen physically. Also, the amount appearing in the Financial Statement has been depreciated while the amount in the inventory report still reflects the original purchase price. Nonetheless, the GSO and Accounting Office will prioritize the reconciliation of balances and for possible disposal of the unserviceable PPEs.
Rejoinder: The basis of the Audit Team in the comparison of the amounts of PPE as per Accounting records and the Inventory Report of the GSO was the acquisition cost and not the depreciated cost as reflected in the submitted Notes to the Financial Statements for PPEs.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant, GSO and Municipal Engineer to exert efforts in locating documents and information pertaining to the road networks of the LGU for CY 2015 and prior years to properly recognize the same in the LGU’s Financial Statements for a fair presentation of the PPE account.
Direct the above-mentioned key officials to accomplish the duties and responsibilities assigned to them; and
Ensure compliance with accounting and reporting guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015.
The Management commented that the Road Networks may have been accounted but was recorded using the wrong accounts. However, the Accounting Office committed to trace these transactions/ documents as basis of rectifying the errors made.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant:
Trace the history of the account;
After tracing, determine the extent of completion of the project/s; and
Draw adjusting and/or correcting entries as necessary.
The Management commented that the amounts of CIP are carried forward and there is difficulty of tracing the history of the account due to unavailability of supporting documents.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Assess the nature of the biological assets held by the Municipality. Thereafter, adopt an accounting policy that best suits the Municipality’s biological assets.
Perform physical count on the biological assets.
Properly include in the Notes to Financial Statements the required disclosures as per IPSAS 27.
The Management commented that the inventory of the Biological Assets was not turned-over to the Municipal Agriculturist.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Locate all available documents regarding the abovementioned accounts in order to prepare the necessary subsidiary ledgers.
Exhaust all possible remedies to reconcile the balances of those payable account in which SL is maintained. Prepare necessary adjusting entries, if any.
The Management commented that the Accounts Payables are carried forward and there is difficulty of tracing the history of the account due to unavailability of supporting documents.

We recommended that the Management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to make appropriate adjustments to correct the abnormal balance in order to report fairly presented financial statements.
Strengthen its internal control over its disbursement process in order to mitigate timing differences in the accounting recognition of asset, liability and expense accounts.
The Management commented that Accounting Office committed to make the necessary corrections/adjusting entries.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant follow the prescribed format in the presentation and disclosure of the Trust Liability – DRRM account.
The Management committed to comply with the audit recommendation.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant:
To draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to immediately transfer the unutilized amount of LDRRMF-QRF and the LRRMF-MOOE for CY 2020 to the special trust fund account.
To ensure that the above entry should always be made annually before the preparation of year-end financial statements.
The Management commented that Accounting Office will coordinate with the Budget Office for the transfer of unutilized amount of LDRRMF to the Trust Fund Book.

We recommended that the municipal accountant should transfer the total amount of accumulated interest income and business tax from the Trust Fund to the General Fund through the following journal entries:
Account Title | Debit | Credit
Trust Fund Books: |  | 
Due to LGUs | 4,024.14 | 
Due to NGAs | 27,344.17 | 
Cash in Bank |  | 31,368.31
General Fund Books: |  | 
Cash in Bank | 31,368.31 | 
Prior Period Adjustment |  | 31,368.31
Account Title | Debit | Credit
Trust Fund Books: |  | 
Cash in Bank | xxx | 
Due to Other Funds (GF) |  | xxx
General Fund Books: |  | 
Due from Other Funds (TF) | xxx | 
Interest Income |  | xxx
The Management committed to comply with the audit recommendations

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Revisit the entry made on Due from Other Funds in CY 2017 amounting to P3,668,601.49 and explain why no counterpart entry on Due to Other Funds was made for the same amount.
Prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and take up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are adjusted on timely basis.
Perform constant review of transactions to deter error and omissions.
The Management commented that the Accounting Department will make the necessary corrections and adjustments.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to avail of the remedies provided under the Rules and Regulations Implementing the Local Government Code of 1991 to enforce collection of RPT.
Also, we recommended that the agency conduct more extensive tax campaign so that landowners/real property owners are apprised of the advantages/services that the Municipality would render if taxes are paid. Develop an efficient and effective monitoring and collection system so as to intensify the collection of its outstanding RPT/SET Receivables.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant determine the extent of recoverability of the outstanding RPT and SET Receivables, and thereafter prepare adjusting entries as necessary to properly value the accounts.
The Management committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Explain the presence of incorrectly classified accounts in the agency’s financial statements; and
Exercise vigilance whenever journal entries are made.

We recommended that management justify why they selected KCX’ Supply Wholesaling as the supplier yet there could be other competitive suppliers with a physical store within the locality or nearby.
The Management commented that the Municipality entered into contract with KCX Supply Wholesaling as the supplier of various goods for COVID-19 related programs/projects/activities; through Negotiated Procurement under Emergency Purchase as mode of procurement because, we found out that it is legally, technically and financially capable supplier for reasons that they have an updated PHILGEPS with the procuring entity or in lieu thereof, they submitted Mayor’s or Business Permits or Business Tax Return and Omnibus Sworn Statement for projects with ABC above P500.00.00. In addition, the supplier offered to deliver the needed goods in full before payment will be made.
Further, the supplier having delivered on time the supplies needed can be considered to be a capable supplier at the time, considering the limited movement for the state was under Enhanced Community Quarantine and that the items were highly needed as the time is of the essence and immediate action was necessary to prevent damage to or loss of life or property, or to restore vital public services, infrastructure facilities and other public utilities.

We recommended that management:
Adopt/install internal control on the transactions of the Municipality to safeguard its asset from wastage or loss.
Should have an updated report/data on the number of household in the Municipality for a more comprehensive plan in the procurement and/or distribution of relief goods.
Require the General Service Officer to accept all procured goods/property of the Municipality. In addition, the Municipality should create an Inspection Committee tasked to inspect all procured goods of the Municipality; and
Henceforth, report on the distribution of relief goods should specify the number/quantity and kind of relief goods distributed to make a comparison between the procured and distributed goods with the goods received by the beneficiary/recipient.
The Management commented that it is the practice of the LGU that all items procured by the LGU is accepted by the end user and inspected by the GSO due to the absence of Inspection Officer/Committee. However, we take notice of the observation and will strictly adhere to the provisions of Appendix 50 of GAM for LGUs by creating Inspection Committee as soon as possible.
In regards with the distribution of relief goods, please be informed that the LGU indicated in the Relief Distribution Sheets the contents of each food packs for the goods purchased with KCX Wholesaling on May 7, 2020 amounting to P7,691,130.00 while all other relief goods purchases has none.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly adhere to the provisions of DBM Local Budget Circular No. 125 on the posting and reporting requirements on Project Implementation of the Bayanihan Grant to Cities and Municipalities (BGCM), for transparency and accountability.
The Management commented that the Municipal Budget Officer consistently submitted monthly the Report on Fund Utilization and Status of PPA to the DBM. In addition, such reports were posted in three conpicuous places namely at the Municipal Hall, LGU Bulletin and the Municipal Auditorium.
Further, the LGU thru its Local Chief Executive (LCE) sent letters to the DBM Regional Office to give update on the projects being conducted. However, the posting of the reports in LGU website was not done because the LGU currently has no existing website.

We commended the Municipality for complying to the recording requirement of DBM Local Budget Circular No. 128 in relation to Local Government Support Fund – Covid-19 Financial Assistance.
However, we recommended that management maximize the utilization of the fund and to implement the same the soonest possible time. Management should fast track the implementation of the fund transfer since the resources are readily available, and in order not to deprive the Municipality of the PPAs where the fund was intended for.
The Management commented that the audit recommendations are well taken. At the start of the year 2021, the Municipal Engineering Office started to make the plan of the said project, but due to the two-week lockdown of our LGU from February 15-28, 2021, the plans/programs of works and other pertinent documents needed, for the implementation is still in progress. Rest assured that the project will be implemented the soonest.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant maintain complete subsidiary ledgers for the special account in the general fund and prepare the corresponding financial reports to form part of the schedules of the general fund trial balance as required under the abovementioned guidelines. This will serve as a tool in informing management on the condition as well as the status of operations of the special account.
The Management committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We commended management for their unparalleled effort and priority in implementing these programs/projects/activities in CY 2020 under the Trust Fund. However the remaining four (4) unimplemented projects which were programmed for the year should be completed without further delay.
The Management commented that the following were the reasons why the remaining four (4) projects were unimplemented at year-end, to wit:
P150,000.00 from DOST as grant for the LGU’s Dairy Industry – the fund was clarified for the intended purposes during the latter part of November 2020. Due to not clear guidelines as to release of funds of such grant and given the narrow time before the year-end, the release of fund was no longer put into action.
P274,331.00 from BTr as grant for assistance to Typhoon Ulysses victims and aftermaths – the funds value date is December 9, 2020 as per the Notice of Authority to Debit Account (NADAI) dated December 10, 2020 which was received by the LGU on later date. Hence, the remaining days for the calendar year was not enough to implement the said project.
Unutilized funds amounting to P2,433.38 from funds transferred to the Municipality were remitted upon completion of the programs/projects/activities, in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 94-013.
Section 4.9 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994,also known as the Rules and Regulations in the Grant, Utilization and Liquidation of Funds Transferred to Implementing Agencies provides that:
“The IA shall return to the SA any unused balance upon completion of the project.”

The Management commented that they appreciate the commendation made by the Audit Team. With regards to the noted unremitted unutilized fund of P15,268.63, the Accounting Office coordinated with the MENRO Designee, and as of date, the documents are ready for its remittance to the BTr. However, the MENRO Designee, sent a request to the Environmental Management Bureau for a permission not to remit the fund for the mean time so the LGU can use such to other projects as addition to future funds. However, until the end of the year, no response was received, hence, the remittance as well as usage of the fund remained or was set to status quo.

We recommended that:
Management explain/justify the non-implementation of the LGSF – Other Financial Assistance despite being received early in 2020.
Moreover, the BAC and other offices that maybe involved in project execution fast track project implementation particularly of the fund transfers. Regular monitoring and evaluation of project implementation should be assigned to specific office so that the desired level of efficiency is achieved and the services and benefits expected from these projects are delivered to the constituents of the Municipality without unnecessary delay.
Also, remit the unutilized amount to the Bureau of the Treasury having failed to implement the project as of December 31, 2020.
Lastly, management should increase aggressiveness in implementing the programs/projects/activities to ensure project implementation performance.
The Management commented that as much as the LGU wanted to fast track the implementation of the above-mentioned project for the benefits of all San Agustinians, and despite all efforts exerted by the implementing body, due to fortuitous events, the project was not put into reality.

Based on the foregoing situation, we recommended the following:
Compensate the inappropriately charged amount in the Trust Fund – LBP Proper account from the Trust Fund – DBP account of P188,020.94.
Stop the practice of using trust funds to pay obligations other than its intended purpose/s.
Be vigilant in the charging of account whenever a disbursement is made in order to conserve money, time and effort and to prevent the occurrence of overdrafts.
The Management commented that error was not intentional and the Accounting Office assured that said error will not happen again. Further, the Accounting Office committed to make the necessary adjusting entries.

We recommended that Management, through its Inventory Committee, conduct complete physical count of properties of the Municipality, reconcile the results of the count with the accounting records and submit the complete and accurate RPCI and RPCPPE to the Audit Team, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the:
MDRRMO (i) see to it that the relevant provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 are strictly adhered to during the preparation of the LDRRMFIP.  He should also coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer regarding the status of the projects classified as CO on previous years in order to present accurate information of continuing projects in the Plan; and (ii) include in the template – Inventory of Equipment Purchased Out of DRRMF/Donations all the equipment purchased both in the current and prior years.  Also, he should see to it that the cost of these equipment were indicated therein.
Municipal Accountant (i) effect the following adjusting entries (1) an amount of P24,000.00 pertaining to the financial assistance given to households affected by Typhoon “Tisoy” and (2) an amount of P784,155.76 in the books of the Trust Fund relative to the fund transfer effected in May 2017 from the Trust Fund - Proper Bank Account No. 1162-1044-18 to General Fund Bank Account No. 1162-1018-26; (ii) comply with Items 4.1 and 5.1.12 of the Circular on the accounting of funds received from the Office of the President; and (iii) strictly adhere to Item 5.1.10 of the Circular which states that all unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE shall be transferred to the Special Trust Fund to be able to present the correct balance of the Trust Liabilities - DRRMF account.  Furthermore, she should properly disclose the amounts and details of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF in the Notes to Financial Statements; and
Municipal Treasurer effect a fund transfer on the following transactions: (i) an amount of P108,000.00 from the Calamity Fund bank account to the Trust Fund - Proper bank account pertaining to the transfer of unutilized balance of DRRMF for CY 2011 to the General Fund erroneously charged against the latter account; (ii) an amount of P45,750.00 from the Calamity Fund bank account to the General Fund bank account pertaining to the purchase of materials for the Rehabilitation of Busay Falls Eco-Tourism Park which was funded from the 70% Mitigation Fund for CY 2015 maintained in the Trust Fund.  This was initially charged against the Trust Fund which the Audit Team found proper.  However, it was later on paid by the General Fund; and (iii) an amount of P64,912.00 from the Calamity Fund bank account to the General Fund bank account for the overstatement of the unexpended balance of 70% Mitigation Fund – MOOE for CY 2017 that was transferred to the Special Trust Fund in CY 2018.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the:
BAC (i) strictly comply with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 in the procurement process undertaken for the projects of the Municipality; and (ii) check the correctness of the information contained in the additional documents submitted by the winning bidder which form part of the contract; and
Municipal Engineer, who acts as the BAC Vice-Chairman and the BAC-TWG Head, see to it that the guidelines provided in the DPWH DO No. 197 series of 2016 were considered during the preparation of ABC.  In addition to the ABC, he should also prepare the documents such as POW, DEE and BOQ with utmost care towards achieving consistency of the information indicated therein.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the:
Procuring Entity strictly comply with Section 17.6 of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9814 to avoid incurring delay in the implementation of projects;
Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant (i) jointly check whether the documentary requirements enumerated in COA Circular No. 2012-001 were fully complied with; (ii) see to it that the amount due to the contractor to be reflected in the disbursement voucher is reconciled with the total value of accomplishments reported in the SWA; and (iii) ensure that the amount of the warranty security posted by the contractor adhere to the guidelines set forth in Section 62.2.3.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184; and
Municipal Engineer (i) inform the contractors that it is their responsibility to prepare and submit the documents such as SWA, Statement of Work Accomplished and Overall Progress Bar Chart and S-Curve; and (ii) thoroughly check the information contained in the SWA, Statement of Time Elapsed and Work Accomplished and Overall Progress Bar Chart and S-Curve to be able to present accurate and reliable information to the users thereof.  He should also pay particular attention on the value of previous accomplishments forwarded in the succeeding SWA in the case of progress payments.  Careful review of the SWA is necessary in evaluating the total value of accomplishments the contractors are entitled to and the corresponding percentages of completion of the projects.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the BAC:
ensure strict compliance with the requirements prescribed under the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to prevent the same lapses in future procurements;
ensure that the information contained in the documents/reports being prepared are consistent; and
ensure the timely submission of the perfected POs and the documents forming part thereof to the Audit Team in compliance with Item 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the:
Municipal Mayor and the concerned accountable officers ensure that all the guidelines provided in the DBM Budget Circular No. 2020-5 are strictly adhered to;
Municipal Accountant ensure that all the documentary requirements are complied with before processing claims.  She shall also be guided by the relevant provisions of the Circular in doing so; and
concerned accountable officer/s submit the lacking document, otherwise, this will be suspended in audit.

We recommended that the GAD TWG Member:
ensure that the minimum budgetary requirement for GAD Fund provided in JMC No. 2013-01 is satisfied.  In doing this, he should properly classify the PPAs as to mandated or attributed programs.  Likewise, he should see to it that the mandated programs identified in the GPB are provided with separate appropriations in the budgets.  He should also coordinate with other offices in conducting gender analysis through the administration of HGDG on their respective programs to be able to determine if portion of their budgets may be attributed to GAD;
carefully prepare the GAD AR with full disclosure of the necessary information for the benefit of the users thereof; and
see to it that the GAD AR is supported with the actions taken by the LGU on the COA audit findings and recommendations.

We recommended Management to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare Journal Entry Voucher (JV) to adjust the properties below the capitalization threshold and its related accounts, as follows:
Account					Debit			Credit
Government Equity 					636,191.23
Accumulated Depreciation Accounts	 1,120,738.14
PPE Accounts						1,756,929.37
To drop non-capitalizable PPEs from CY 1991 to 2020
Supplies and Materials Expense 		   231,155.00
Depreciation Expense					   225,665.67
Surplus (Deficit) Account 			      5,489.33
To reclassify the non-capitalizable properties acquired in CY 2020
Ensure that all future acquisitions of properties below the capitalization threshold of ₱15,000 will be recorded in accordance with Section 5.4 of COA Circular 2016-006 and Chapter II Section 4.ee of GAM for LGU Volume I.

We recommended Management to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Adjust the misstatement of the Accumulated Depreciation accordingly to rectify the errors of calculation and to present the PPE account to its correct carrying amount;
Consistently review the correctness of the computation of depreciation and appropriately maintain PPE accounts in adherence to the latest provisions; and
Refer to and seek approval from the Office of the Auditor in cases where a more appropriate residual value is determined by the LGU and shall be adopted, as prescribed in Items h and i, Section 262 of GAM for LGUs.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Direct the Inventory Committee to prepare and submit the approved Physical Inventory Plan to the COA Audit Team at least ten (10) calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory-taking activities; and
Ensure the full implementation of "One-time cleansing of PPE accounts" as provided under COA Circular 2020-006.

We recommended Management to direct the Municipal Treasurer to:
Locate/obtain the documents of the motor vehicles and facilitate the processing of registrations and insurance coverage of the motor vehicles; and
Regularly monitor the expiration and renewal of all motor vehicles registration to avoid penalties or fines for late/non-registration.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure on-time remittance of barangay shares from RPT collections in the succeeding quarters as provided under Section 271 (d) of the Local Government Code.

6.8	We recommended Management to instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
6.8.1	Ensure that the unremitted barangay shares from tourism fees for CY 2019 and CY 2020 will be immediately transferred to the respective barangays; and
6.8.2	Formulate mechanism that will ensure that the barangay shares from tourism fees will be remitted promptly, moving forward.

We recommended Management to:
Require the Local Finance Committee to ensure that the financial information of Bayanihan Grant to Cities and Municipalities are made available to the public, in adherence to the prescribed guidelines outlined in the Local Budget Circular No. 125;
Require the Administrative Officer to expedite the creation of the Municipal Government's Official Website; and
Require the Administrative Officer to ensure proper maintenance of all Bulletin Boards in the designated conspicuous public places within the Municipality.
The Management commented that LGU San Agustin is now appropriating the corresponding budget for the installation of the glass panel and other protective measures to secure the bulletin boards, especially those in the designated conspicuous public places. The Management has already instructed the Information Officer to fast track the creation of a Municipal Website to maximize its posting capabilities, especially for those reportorial requirements that require timely delivery of information to both the oversight parties and the general public.

We recommended Management to direct the MDRRM Officer to:
Update themselves to the latest policies and guidelines on Donations In-Kind as provided by the relevant and competent government offices; and
Ensure the on-time preparation and submission of the Report of the Donations Received, Distributed and Balances to the Office of the Civil Defense and Auditor’s Office, in compliance to Section 2(b) of COA Circular 2020-009, moving forward.

10.7	We recommended Management to direct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant:
10.7.1    to be meticulous in the acceptance of documentary requirements for all government procurement, and be updated with the guidelines provided by the relevant and competent government offices; and
10.7.2    ensure that the Omnibus Sworn Statements (OSS) from the Suppliers are notarized before processing the payments.

We recommended Management to:
Direct the BAC Secretariat to immediately comply with the GPPB Online Portal's posting requirement for Emergency Procurement under Republic Act 11469 (Bayanihan to Heal as One Act);
Capacitate the Members of the BAC Secretariat on the laws, rules and regulations on government procurement; and
Require the BAC Secretariat to promote transparency in government procurement by ensuring that the Notices of Award and Purchase Orders for future procurements are posted in accordance with the guidelines prescribed.

We recommended Management to require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the required withholding taxes are deducted before making any payment to the suppliers, and to remit the same to the BIR within the prescribed period in compliance with Section 272 of Republic Act 8424.

13.7	We recommended Management to instruct the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the provisions of the law regarding the mandated monthly net take-home pay for government employees to boost the morale and productivity of its employees in government service.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to evaluate and determine without delay the transactions which caused the variance and effect reconciliation thereof and to perform the same regularly, pursuant to Section 181 (c) of the GAAM, Volume 1, to present fairly the cash balances in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Cash book balance and Financial Statement balance were reconciled
Liquidations for unliquidated balance of Advances for Payroll for CY 2019 were made. However, there are balances of CY 2020 that were not yet liquidated at year-end.
observations.reason: Reiterated under Finding No 3.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant, GSO and Municipal Engineer to exert efforts in locating documents and information pertaining to the road networks of the LGU for CY 2015 and prior years to properly recognize the same in the LGU’s Financial Statements for a fair presentation of the PPE account.
Direct the above-mentioned key officials to accomplish the duties and responsibilities assigned to them; and
Ensure compliance with accounting and reporting guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015.
observations.managementAction: Local Road Networks for CY 2016-2020 are recorded in the book of accounts.
observations.reason:
Local Road Networks for CY 2019 and previous years still not recorded in the book of accounts.

Reiterated under Finding No. 10

observations.recommendation: We therefore reiterated our recommendation that the agency record its receipt and disbursements of fund transfers under the specific account as prescribed under the Revised Chart of Accounts for the proper monitoring of transactions relating thereon. Further, reclassify the account “Due to NGAs” to “Due to LGUs amounting to P20,000,000.00.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office prepared the adjusting entries to correct the error.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management stop using government funds for advertising expenses in newspapers for purposes other than what is allowed in the aforementioned COA Circular.
observations.managementAction: No expenses for advertisement for CY 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management stop granting honorarium to those designated PESO Managers. Likewise, the amounts of P42,000.00 and P14,000.00 should be refunded as there was no legal basis for granting thereto. Also, the management should comply with the provisions of LBC No. 62 on the granting of honoraria to officials and employees of the LGU.
observations.managementAction: The honoraria granted to PESO Officer was discontinued for CY 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management strictly comply with the provisions of the Executive Order 77 particularly on the allowable rates for transportation and miscellaneous expenses and Daily Travel Expense (DTE).
observations.managementAction: The claims for transportation are in accordance with the prescribed rate under EO No. 77.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the agency submit the lacking supporting documents for the contract review of the infrastructure projects to avoid suspension/ disallowance.

Also, in the future, inorder to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit,  we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC Secretariat to attach all supporting documents of perfected contracts as required in the Revised IRR of RA 9184, arranged in chronological manner, and furnish the Auditor within five (5) working days upon approval together with the supporting documents for review.
observations.managementAction: Perfected contracts were submitted to COA Office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management adhere strictly to the provisions of the 2016 IRR of RA 9184 on the preparation and posting of the Annual Procurement Plan for better procurement planning and budgeting linkage.
Also, we recommended the agency to strictly adhere to the posting requirements of the APP for control and transparency.
observations.managementAction: APP prepared for CY 2020 adhered with the IRR of RA 9184.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the agency submit the lacking supporting documents of the billings as indicated to avoid suspension of the said disbursements.

Also, in the future, inorder to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit,  we reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:
direct the Municipal Accountant not to process claims of  progress billing/final payment unless  all the required supporting documents are present and attached to the disbursement vouchers; and
ensure strict compliance to Section 9.1.1.1 to 9.1.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated  June 14, 2012.
observations.managementAction: Lacking Documents were submitted. Supporting Documents for payments of infrastructure projects for CY 2020 are complete.

observations.recommendation:
We commended management for their unparalleled effort in implementing the 
LGSF - FA program however, the noted deficiencies that needs to be improved be taken into consideration.

We commended management for their partial compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 94-013 particularly on the handling of unutilized funds however, the noted deficiency that needs to be implemented be undertaken.

We commended management for continuously complying with all the regulations pertaining to the allocation, appropriation and programming  of 20% from its Internal Revenue Allotment (IRA) for development fund and its strict adherence to the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2017-01.

We recommended that management ensure that PAPS funded out of the 20% DF are implemented within the period specified in the AIP. For PAPs that were still unimplemented and on-going, positive efforts should be made to fast track the completion of the said PAPs. In addition, submit explanation on the delay of completion of the PAPs most especially those under the CYs 2016-2017.

We recommended to management that projects/ programs intended for implementation during the year must be implemented considering that there was fund allocation for that project. Also, submit an explanation why the said program was started only on December 2019.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Enforce the full settlement of the above-listed disallowances by the persons liable; and
Ensure compliance with COA rules on settlement of accounts.

We recommended that the LGU shall appropriate in its annual budget at least one percent (1%) of the Internal Revenue Allotment for the Local Council for the Protection of Children in order to meet the needs of the children specially those in conflict with the law and the children who are at risk.

We recommended the management to charge the financial assistance to general fund rather than to the GAD fund. Also, instead of giving financial assistance, the LGU should disburse to a more concrete or specific and attainable project, programs or services that would address gender issues and refrain from charging financial assistance because these would deplete the budget for intended activities.

We recommended that the Management, through its Inventory Committee, conduct complete physical count of the properties of the Municipality, reconcile the results of the count with the accounting records and submit the complete and accurate RPCI and RPCPPE to the Audit Team, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.
observations.managementAction: The Management conducted physical count of inventories and properties at year-end.
observations.reason:
Comparison with the balances of the Inventories accounts presented in the financial statements vis-à-vis the totals per recomputation of the Audit Team on the amounts reflected in the RPCI disclosed an unreconciled difference amounting to P61,155.04 due to erroneous computation of the totals of the items reflected in the report and absence of the quantities per card and per count on some of the inventories.  On the other hand, no RPCPPE was submitted to the Audit Team.  During the exit conference, the Management commented that the conduct of physical count of properties was not completed.

Reiterated in Observation No. 1 of this report.

observations.managementAction:
In February 2020, the Municipal Accountant effected a fund transfer amounting to P2,881,546.27 from LBP Account No. 1162-1084-80 to LBP Account No. 1162-1018-26 to cover the expenditures erroneously charged against the latter account.  She has also exerted efforts in the preparation of the List of Vouchers/
Projects for 20% DF as there is only one instance when a particular disbursement was excluded therein.  The Municipal Accountant also explained during the exit conference that MOOE accounts were used in the recording of disbursements on projects that will later on be turned over to the barangays.  The appropriate asset accounts were used in the recording thereof in the books of the barangays.
observations.reason:
Other lapses were still observed in CY 2020 audit. 

Reiterated in Observation No. 4 of this report.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the BAC: 

ensure strict compliance with the requirements prescribed under the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to prevent the same lapses in future procurements;

ensure that the information contained in the documents/
reports being prepared were consistent;

carefully check the additional documents submitted by the winning bidder which will form part of the contract to achieve consistency of information; and

ensure the timely submission of the perfected contracts and the documents forming part thereof to the Audit Team in compliance with Item 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer, being the personnel in charge in the implementation of infrastructure projects, see to it that all the required information on the projects were indicated in the billboards installed within the construction premises.  We further recommended that he shall oversee the implementation of the projects and ensure that these are implemented in accordance with the timeline set by the Procuring Entity.

observations.recommendation:
The Audit Team also verified the compliance of the BAC with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and observed that: 

The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) committed procedural lapses in the procurement activities undertaken for the projects charged against the 20% DF which were not in accordance with the provisions of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of Republic Act (R.A.) No. 9184, otherwise known as the Government Procurement Reform Act, thus, affecting the propriety of the contracts. 

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

direct the MPDC to appraise the socio-economic and environmental conditions of the locality and identify the programs and activities to be given priority to address the necessity of the constituents.

direct the MPDC and the Municipal Engineer to see to it that the projects to be charged against the 20% Development Fund are in line with the allowable development projects enumerated in the DILG and DBM JMC No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 amended by JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017. 

instruct the Municipal Accountant to carefully prepare the List of Vouchers Against the Fund to accurately present the relevant information.

direct the concerned personnel to properly coordinate in the preparation of various reports for consistency of information. 



We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to: 

strictly comply with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184;

judiciously check the documents submitted by the bidders; 

be cautious in preparing documents to show consistency of information to the readers of the documents; and

submit to the Audit Team the copies of Purchase Orders and the supporting documents within the period prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 to avoid incurrence of delay.
observations.managementAction:
All the projects identified in the AIP for CY 2020 were in accordance with Item 3.0 of the JMC.  The Municipal Accountant also ensured reconciliation of the appropriations indicated in the List of Vouchers/
Projects for the 20% DF in CY 2020 vis-à-vis the ending balances appearing in the List of Vouchers/
Projects for the 20% DF in CY 2019.  Furthermore, the Municipal Treasurer had already transferred the amount pertaining to the 20% DF to its own bank account in March 2019.  Out of the total appropriations amounting to P21,000,000.00, projects with appropriations totaling P5,600,000.00 equivalent to 26.67% were not implemented during CY 2020.  This only showed that the Municipality see to it that majority of the PPAs charged against the 20% DF were implemented during the year.  Also, during the exit conference, the Municipal Engineer has cited reasons for the non-implementation thereof which the Audit Team found valid.  Furthermore, the Municipal Accountant explained during the exit conference that MOOE accounts were used in the recording of disbursements on projects that will later on be turned over to the barangays.  The appropriate asset accounts were used in the recording thereof in the books of the barangays.

The GFPS ensured the correctness of the scores provided in the HGDG checklists attached in the GPB and the GAD AR.  









The BAC has already addressed majority of the observations in the procurement of goods undertaken in CY 2020 which constitutes substantial compliance.
observations.reason:
In CY 2020, the 5% minimum requirement for GAD Fund was not met during the implementation phase due to the following circumstances: (a) reduction in the budget allocated to some GAD activities and (b) exclusion of the GAD activity – Purchase and Distribution of Comfort Room Materials and Allocation of Labor Assistance to Indigent Households.  Furthermore, the GAD AR was not supported with the actions taken by the LGU on the COA audit findings and recommen-dations.       

Reiterated in Observation No. 9 of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

exert more effort for timely preparation and submission of the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements;

obtain the necessary documents to support the reconciling items identified and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balance of Cash, LCCA in the books of the Agency; and

maintain subsidiary ledgers of all bank accounts.
observations.managementAction: Verification of the BRSs for the month of December 2020 disclosed that there were book reconciling items on that month.  Since the bank statements were received by the Municipal Accountant only on March 24, 2021, the debit and credit memos were not recorded in the books on the same month.  The Audit Team likewise observed that                                                  the Municipal Accountant exerted efforts in the early submission to the Audit Team of the BRSs upon receipt of the bank statements.
observations.reason: Comparison between the unadjusted book balances of the bank accounts maintained in the Trust Fund appearing in the BRSs for the month of December vis-à-vis the balance of the Cash in Bank - Local Currency, Current Account as at December 31, 2020 shown in the financial statements disclosed a discrepancy amounting to P904,791.26.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management exert effort in determining if the National Solid Waste Management Commission approved their 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan.   Moreover, comply strictly with the provisions of Republic Act No. 9003.
observations.managementAction:
The Management has demonstrated various actions relative to Solid Waste Management.  During CY 2018, the Municipality purchased a lot intended for sanitary landfill.  Furthermore, two units of three-wheeled cargo vehicles for Barangays Cabolutan and Lusong were procured by the Municipality in the same year to be used in the collection of solid wastes in the concerned barangays.  The Municipality likewise purchased two units of mini dump trucks during CY 2018.  In CY 2019, the Municipality purchased various equipment for the Central Materials Recovery Facility (MRF) namely: sorting conveyor with hopper, rapid composter, waste pulverizer, waste crusher, waste mixer and paving block machine press with a total cost of P7,650,000.00.  The projects – Construction of Central MRF for 15 Barangays and Construction of Multi-Purpose Building for MRF Equipment/
Machineries (Phase I) were also implemented during the year.  In CY 2020, the Municipality has implemented the projects namely: Completion of Central MRF at Barangay Binongaan and Purchase and Installation of Equipment for the Central MRF as well as the Purchase of three-wheeled vehicle for Solid Waste Management Program in Barangay Carmen.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare subsidiary ledgers for each trust liability and to reconcile the same to the reported amount on the book of accounts thereby fairly presenting the balances to the financial statement and to facilitate the reversion of the unexpended balance of the completed projects to the National Treasury for utilization to other developmental projects.
We recommended that management require the persons liable in the transaction to refund the disallowance of P1,372,202.00 which excess cost of the dumptrucks as against COA evaluated cost.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant should reflect in the agency books the valid reconciling items noted in the Bank Reconciliation Statements. After adjustments, the books and the financial statements should conform to the adjusted cash balance per bank reconciliation as of December 31, 2019.
We further recommended that the Accounting Office and Municipal Treasurer should periodically reconcile their records.
We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to evaluate and determine without delay the transactions which caused the variance and effect reconciliation thereof and to perform the same regularly, pursuant to Section 181 (c) of the GAAM, Volume 1, to present fairly the cash balances in the financial statements.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Locate all available documents regarding the abovementioned accounts in order to prepare the necessary subsidiary ledgers.
Exhaust all possible remedies to locate records or documents of the balances of long outstanding receivables in order to require the concerned agencies/organizations to immediately cause the liquidation and settlement of the same and/or request for the write-off of dormant accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accounting Office record all purchases to its corresponding inventory account classification and to adjust the account for whatever consumption on the basis of Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI), duly supported with the original copy of Requisition Issue Slip (RIS), which should be prepared and submitted weekly or monthly to the OIC-Municipal Accountant.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management:
Prioritize the reconciliation of balances between the accounting records and RPCPPE. Prepare adjusting entries, if any, to reflect the correct balance of the affected accounts.
Should consider disposing the unserviceable PPE, after reconciliation was made, and derecognize such from the Agency’s book of accounts.
Maintain a detailed Equipment Ledger Card for each class of Property, Plant and Equipment that supports the balances presented in the FS. The schedule should enable the user to trace for the acquisition date and cost of an individual PPE to facilitate depreciation computation.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant, GSO and Municipal Engineer to exert efforts in locating documents and information pertaining to the road networks of the LGU for CY 2015 and prior years to properly recognize the same in the LGU’s Financial Statements for a fair presentation of the PPE account.
Direct the above-mentioned key officials to accomplish the duties and responsibilities assigned to them; and
Ensure compliance with accounting and reporting guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Assess the nature of the biological assets held by the Municipality. Thereafter, adopt an accounting policy that best suits the Municipality’s biological assets.
Perform physical count on the biological assets.
Properly include in the Notes to Financial Statements the required disclosures as per IPSAS 27.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Locate all available documents regarding the abovementioned accounts in order to prepare the necessary subsidiary ledgers.
Exhaust all possible remedies to reconcile the balances of those payable account in which SL is maintained. Prepare necessary adjusting entries, if any.

We therefore reiterated our recommendation that the agency record its receipt and disbursements of fund transfers under the specific account as prescribed under the Revised Chart of Accounts for the proper monitoring of transactions relating thereon. Further, reclassify the account “Due to NGAs” to “Due to LGUs amounting to P20,000,000.00.
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant prepares financial statements to include and provide the requirements called for under the aforementioned circular.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Revisit the entry made on Due from Other Funds in CY 2017 and explain why no counterpart entry on Due to Other Funds was made.
Prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and take up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are adjusted on timely basis.
Perform constant review of transactions to deter error and omissions.
We recommended that Management stop using government funds for advertising expenses in newspapers for purposes other than what is allowed in the aforementioned COA Circular.

We recommended that management stop granting honorarium to those designated PESO Managers. Likewise, the amounts of P42,000.00 and P14,000.00 should be refunded as there was no legal basis for granting thereto. Also, the management should comply with the provisions of LBC No. 62 on the granting of honoraria to officials and employees of the LGU.

We recommended that management strictly comply with the provisions of the Executive Order 77 particularly on the allowable rates for transportation and miscellaneous expenses and Daily Travel Expense (DTE).

We recommended that the agency submit the lacking supporting documents for the contract review of the infrastructure projects to avoid suspension/ disallowance.
Also, in the future, inorder to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit,  we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC Secretariat to attach all supporting documents of perfected contracts as required in the Revised IRR of RA 9184, arranged in chronological manner, and furnish the Auditor within five (5) working days upon approval together with the supporting documents for review.

We recommended that management adhere strictly to the provisions of the 2016 IRR of RA 9184 on the preparation and posting of the Annual Procurement Plan for better procurement planning and budgeting linkage.
Also, we recommended the agency to strictly adhere to the posting requirements of the APP for control and transparency.

We reiterated our recommendation that the agency submit the lacking supporting documents of the billings as indicated to avoid suspension of the said disbursements.
Also, in the future, inorder to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit,  we reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:
direct the Municipal Accountant not to process claims of  progress billing/final payment unless  all the required supporting documents are present and attached to the disbursement vouchers; and
ensure strict compliance to Section 9.1.1.1 to 9.1.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated  June 14, 2012.

We commended management for their unparalleled effort in implementing the 
LGSF - FA program however, the noted deficiencies that needs to be improved be taken into consideration.

We commended management for their partial compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 94-013 particularly on the handling of unutilized funds however, the noted deficiency that needs to be implemented be undertaken.

We recommended that management ensure that PAPs funded out of the 20% DF are implemented within the period specified in the AIP. For PAPs that were still unimplemented and on-going, positive efforts should be made to fast track the completion of the said PAPs. In addition, submit explanation on the delay of completion of the PAPs most especially those under the CYs 2016-2017.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant maintain complete subsidiary ledgers for the 20% Development Fund in the general fund and prepare the corresponding financial reports to form part of the schedules of the general fund trial balance as required under the abovementioned guidelines.

We recommended to management that projects/programs intended for implementation during the year must be implemented considering that there was fund allocation for that project. Also, submit an explanation why the said program was started only on December 2019.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Enforce the full settlement of the above-listed disallowances by the persons liable; and
Ensure compliance with COA rules on settlement of accounts.

We recommended that the LGU shall appropriate in its annual budget at least one percent (1%) of the Internal Revenue Allotment for the Local Council for the Protection of Children in order to meet the needs of the children specially those in conflict with the law and the children who are at risk.

We recommended the management to appropriate the LCPC funds to projects or programs that is more responsive to the needs of the children, specially those children at risk and children in conflict with the law, rather than allocating the majority of LCPC fund to payment of honoraria. Also, always act for the best interest of the children as the LCPC fund was created.

We recommended the management to charge the financial assistance to general fund rather than to the GAD fund. Also, instead of giving financial assistance, the LGU should disburse to a more concrete or specific and attainable project, programs or services that would address gender issues and refrain from charging financial assistance because these would deplete the budget for intended activities.

We recommended that the Management, through its Inventory Committee, conduct complete physical count of the properties of the Municipality, reconcile the results of the count with the accounting records and submit the complete and accurate RPCI and RPCPPE to the Audit Team, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.

We recommended that the LDRRMO undertake the following actions: (a) ensure that the current appropriations incorporated in the LDRRMFIP were reconciled with the appropriations provided in the budgets in order to arrive at the correct amount of unexpended balances of LDRRMF; (b) keep track of the projects with continuing appropriations since these should be considered in the preparation of the Plan.  Proper monitoring of these projects will also serve as a control measure in the disbursements to be charged therefrom; (c) stopped the practice of providing lump-sum appropriations using the generic term “Capital Outlay” in the Plan since only the allocation for 30% QRF shall be presented in lump-sum appropriations; (d) submit to the Audit Team the supplemental LDRRMFIP for the projects funded from the unexpended balances of 30% QRF for CYs 2014 to 2018 and the savings from the 70% Mitigation Fund – MOOE for CY 2019 if indeed it was prepared before disbursements were made; (e) properly classify the projects to be charged against the Calamity Fund to arrive at the correct amounts of unexpended balances of 70% Mitigation Fund – MOOE and CO; and (f) include all the equipment purchased out of DRRMF (both current year and prior years) in the template – Inventory of Equipment Purchased Out of DRRMF/Donations as of December 31, 2019.

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the breakdown of the Training Expenses amounting to P92,544.00 which formed part of the total utilization reported in CY 2017.  At the same time, the LDRRMO shall provide the Audit Team on the source of fund used in the disbursements totaling P78,297.00 for the Fabrication of Collapsible Tents.    This is to be undertaken to be able to present the correct amounts of unexpended balance of 70% Mitigation Fund – MOOE for CYs 2015 and 2016.  The Municipal Accountant and the LDRRMO shall also coordinate during the preparation of the related documents/reports towards achieving reconciliation of the amounts and consistency of information.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) transfer back to the Trust Fund the unexpended balances of 30% QRF for CYs 2017 and 2018 amounting to P182,822.40 and P1,418,762.63, respectively, since these are still available for use in disaster risk reduction and management activities on the succeeding years until such time that the five-year period already lapsed.  Relative to this, a fund transfer amounting to P5,996,303.03 shall be made from the Calamity Fund bank account to the Trust Fund – Proper bank account; (b) ensure that the service provider had already performed its services before payments were made thereto; (c) cause a fund transfer amounting to P108,000.00 from the Calamity Fund bank account to the Trust Fund – Proper bank account pertaining to the unutilized balance of DRRMF for CY 2011 reverted to the General Fund which was taken from the latter account; (d) account the unexpended balance of 30% QRF and 70% Mitigation Fund – MOOE for CY 2019 and immediately transfer to the Special Trust Fund in compliance with Item 5.1.10 of the Circular; (e) see to it that all the disbursements pertaining to the projects charged against the 5% Calamity Fund were accounted in the List of Vouchers/Projects for 5% Calamity Fund, Report on Utilization of DRRMF and the disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements to achieve correct amount of unexpended balances of LDRRMF; (f) make a fund transfer amounting to P45,750.00 from Trust Fund to General Fund pertaining to the purchase of materials for the project – Rehabilitation of Busay Falls Eco-Tourism Park which should be charged against the former fund; and (g) carefully prepare the disclosure on the amounts and details of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF in the Notes to Financial Statements to provide the users thereof with complete and accurate information.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer along with other members of the BAC properly plan the procurement schedule on the projects implemented by administration to prevent the situations wherein the projects were already completed even before the delivery of the construction materials.  The Municipal Engineer is also advised to commence the actual construction of the projects once the needed materials were already delivered by the winning suppliers.

We recommended that the:
Local Finance Committee and the members of the GFPS coordinate to ensure that the 5% minimum budgetary requirement for GAD Fund is strictly complied with to be able to address the gender issues present in the locality;
members of the GFPS see to it that the appropriations separately provided in the budgets are enough to support the mandated programs identified in the GPB; and
GFPS TWG carefully prepare the GAD AR including the attachments thereon.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the BAC:
ensure strict compliance with the requirements prescribed under the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to prevent the same lapses in future procurements;
ensure that the information contained in the documents/reports being prepared were consistent; and
ensure the timely submission of the perfected POs and the documents forming part thereof to the Audit Team in compliance with Item 3.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the:
Municipal Mayor and the concerned accountable officers ensure that all the guidelines provided in the DBM Budget Circular No. 2019-5 are strictly adhered to;
Municipal Accountant ensure that all the documentary requirements are complied with before processing claims.  She shall also be guided by the relevant provisions of the Circular in doing so; and
concerned accountable officer/s submit all the lacking documents mentioned in this observation, otherwise, these will be suspended in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, being the HoA, be properly guided by the provisions of the JC No. 2015-01 to be able to act accordingly in the budgeting, release, utilization and reporting of the CF.  We further recommended that he facilitate the submission of the Quarterly Accomplishment Report to the DILG Secretary in compliance with the JC.  The same should be submitted to the Audit Team.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the:
Local Finance Committee identify additional sources of income;
Municipal Treasurer (i) determine doable measures to improve collection efficiency of internally-sourced taxes, fees and charges; (ii) collect the arrearages of SAINTR, Inc..  Likewise, impose surcharges upon it as well as to the other defaulting stallholders for the delayed payments; and (iii) update the master list of stallholders by indicating the abovementioned vital information; and
Local Chief Executive execute contract of lease with the 48 stallholders.  As to the stallholders who have an existing contract with the Municipality, cause the execution of a new contract wherein the rental fee to be reflected therein shall be in conformity with the 2017 Revenue Code.  Also, the contract should indicate the duration thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant  review the applicable accounting standard on the recognition/derecognition principle of the property, plant and equipment and disclose the missing PPE in the notes to financial statements to provide adequate information for the users of the financial statements.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to present the current and non-current portion of its Loans Payable in the face of the LGU’s financial statements and provide information of its amortization in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

The Management acknowledged our observation and assured us that they will comply with the recommendations.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Treasurer renew all PARs that reached the prescribed period of three years, come up with a systematic filing of the documents preferably in alphabetical sequence for easy access and reference as well as indicate in the PAR any subsequent transfer of an asset from one accountable officer to another.

We recommended that Management strictly impose the collection of the balances from water and rental fees, enforce disconnection of water supply if the delinquent resident failed to pay the amount due, and ensure that the official receipts covering payments are presented prior to the release of the equipment in compliance to the municipal ordinance.

The Management acknowledged our observation and assured us that they will comply with the recommendations. The disconnection of water supply for those delinquent residents will be implemented after the public hearing for the new proposed rate of water services which will be on the 1st - 2nd quarter of CY2020. After the said activity, the management will immediately send disconnection notice.

We recommended that Management maintain a separate account for the operations of Municipal EEDM and Municipal Memorial Park as required under Item 5 of the Local Budget Circular (LBC) 111 – Manual on the Setting Up and Operation of Local Economic Enterprise.

The Management acknowledged our observation and assured us that they will comply with the recommendation.

We recommended that Management assign additional personnel who will focus on the preparation of the contracts and its supporting documents and submit these to the Office not later than five (5) working days from the issuance of PO and perfection of contracts for auditorial review and evaluation so that any deficiencies noted can be communicated to the management immediately.

The Management acknowledged our observation and assured us that they will comply with the recommendations.

We recommended that Management ensure completeness and propriety of the required supporting documents for all contracts submitted to COA in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

The Management acknowledged our observation and assured us that they will comply with the recommendations.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to strictly monitor the compliance on the timely payment of the loan amortizations; submit a written explanation justifying the incurrence of the cumulative penalties; and advice the Municipal Accountant to record financial transactions in its appropriate account classification.

The Management acknowledged our observation and assured us that they will comply with the recommendations.

With respect to the paid and unpaid penalties, the Management commented that at the inception of implementing Sub-Project Loan Agreement (SLPA) between MDFO and LGU San Agustin, they were guided with the Tentative Statement of Annual Amortization given by MDFO dated September 19, 2013 with total obligation of ₱ 25,053,242.86. The LGU did not receive changes in the amortization, thus, the Management continued following the said amortization as originally given.
They also commented that they were informed with another Statement of Annual Amortization dated July 5, 2018 showing a total of ₱27,510,540.69. Since it was only printed in July 2018, the amount used for appropriation for loan payment in 2019 was still based on the tentative schedule in 2013. Thus, the payments made to MDFO from 2013 to 2019 were different from that of what had been given in July 2018, and so penalties were incurred by the LGU without knowing of such penalties.
The Management also added that upon payment of amortization directly to MDFO during 2019, they were given with a Statement of Annual Amortization dated April 8, 2019. It was during that time that they found out that their payments to MDFO had been partly applied to the penalties and not to the principal. With this, the Management conferred the matter to the MDFO management and was required to submit justification paper as their basis of condonation of penalties. They are now in the process of preparing justification letter seeking condonation of said penalties and refund of payments made.

We recommended and Management agreed to strictly follow the provisions under Section 272 of the Local Government Code and DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 01, Series of 2017 in the utilization of the SEF.

The Management acknowledged our observation and assured us that they will comply with the recommendation.

We recommended that the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office maximize the utilization of the LDRRM funds and fully implement the programs, projects and activities to achieve the intended purpose of the fund.

The Management acknowledged our observation and assured us to comply with the recommendations.

The Management also commented that one major contributing factor for the low utilization was the procurement of rescue vehicle with an Approved Budget Contract (ABC) of ₱1,600,000.00 which was declared failure of bidding.

We recommended that Management require the GAD Focal Point to include in the submission of the GAD AR the reports/documents mentioned in Section 4.C.8.5 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular 2013-01 to facilitate the monitoring, evaluation and substantiation of the reported accomplishments.

The Management acknowledged our observation and assured us that they will comply with the recommendation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor reconstitute and/or renew the new set of BAC members annually and the BAC to create the Technical Working Group from the pool of technical, financial and/or legal experts to assist in the procurement process pursuant to the provisions of Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

The Management acknowledged our observation and assured us that they will comply with the recommendation.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant adjust the Municipality’s share at P100.00 per month per permanent employee whose compensation is ₱5,000.00 or above per month as mandated by Republic Act No. 9679.

The Municipal Accountant acknowledged our observation and assured us that they will comply with the recommendation.

We recommended that the agency should reflect in the agency books the ₱9,310,397.86 as adjustments in the affected accounts. After adjustments, the books and the financial statements should conform to the adjusted cash balance per bank reconciliation as of December 31, 2018.
observations.managementAction:
The reconciling items for CY 2018 were adjusted in the book of accounts.

However, 2019 reconciling items were not adjusted in the book of accounts hence reiterated under Observation No. 1

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to evaluate and determine without delay the transactions which caused the variance and effect reconciliation thereof and to perform the same regularly, pursuant to Section 181 (c) of the GAAM, Volume I, to present fairly the cash balances in the financial statements.
