a.	Require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to cause the field collecting officer to restitute the remaining balance of P169,000.00 immediately and relieve him of his duties as collecting officer.

b.	Sanctions against erring accountable officer must be imposed as provided under Article 217, Revised Penal Code which provides that “The failure of the accountable officer to have duly forthcoming any public funds or property with which he is chargeable, upon demand by a duly authorized officer, shall be a prima facie evidence that he has put such missing funds or property to personal use.

c.	Require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to personally monitor the receipt and issuance of accountable forms to designated collecting officers through properly accomplished DSCAF/RAAF.
observations.managementAction: The total amount of P279,000.00 was already restituted. System of monitoring for issuance of accountable officer was already adopted by the OIC-Municipal Treasurer

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that management require the Municipal Accountant to:

a.	Issue demand letters to notify the concerned officials and employees of their responsibility to settle their unliquidated cash advances. If no settlement was made, withhold from their salaries considering that they have been notified and given adequate time to liquidate.

b.	Issue demand letters to employees who already resigned or transferred to other offices for the immediate liquidation or settlement of the unliquidated balances

c.	Request for the write-off of the unliquidated balance of the deceased employees, when applicable.

d.	Reclassify “Other Receivables” account to Advances to Officers and Employees account in order to present fairly the total balance of unsettled cash advances.

e.	Ensure the completeness of prescribed supporting documents for the granting of cash advances before processing claims for payment thereof.

f.	Not grant additional cash advance to concerned officials and employees unless the previous cash advance granted to them have been liquidated.

g.	Strictly monitor periodically the submission of liquidation reports to ensure timely accounting thereof to avoid undue accumulation of outstanding cash advances.

h.	Strictly comply with the provisions of the abovementioned Circulars.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant is strict in granting of cash advances. Most of the current year cash advances were already liquidated. Most of the unliquidated balances were from previous year. The agency did not issue demand letters on CY 2019 and no request for write off was made.
observations.reason: Reiterated under Observation No. 3 in Part II of this report

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to identify the cause or reason of the differences and make the necessary adjusting entries, if any, to be able to present fairly the balance of Advances to Officers and Employees account in the Financial Statement.

c.	Maintain a detailed Equipment Ledger Card for each class of Property, Plant and Equipment that supports the balances presented in the FS. The schedule should enable the user to trace for the acquisition date and cost of an individual PPE to facilitate depreciation computation.
observations.managementAction: There is a decrease in the unreconciled differences

Also, we recommended that the LGU device an effective and efficient way of determining beneficiaries of the LGSF – FA in order to maximize the purpose of the fund.

We recommended that the agency submit the required supporting documents for the abovementioned expenditures to avoid suspension in audit of the said disbursements.

Also, in the future, in order to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant not to process claims of payment unless all the required supporting documents are present and attached to the disbursement vouchers.

We recommended that Management:

Ensure that the utilization of Development Fund must be for capital expenditure and/or in the nature of investments; and

Strict adherence with the DILG and DBM joint Circular No. 2017-1 in the preparation of Annual Investment Plan to be funded out of the 20% EDF to ensure that all items to be funded are development programs/projects and activities which are compliant with the guidelines.

Enforce the full settlement of the above-listed disallowances by the persons liable; and

Ensure compliance with COA rules on settlement of accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management, through its Inventory Committee, conduct complete physical count of the properties of the Municipality, reconcile the results of the count with the accounting records and submit the complete and accurate RPCI and RPCPPE to the Audit Team, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.
observations.managementAction: The Management conducted physical count of the properties at year-end.
observations.reason:
Comparison made between the RPCI and the accounting records revealed an unreconciled difference amounting to P95,654.00.  The same is true with the reconciliation made by the Audit Team between the RPCPPE and the accounting records.  An unreconciled difference amounting to P55,846,610.78 resulted from the comparison.   These showed that the Municipality still failed to conduct complete physical count of the properties at year-end.  

Reiterated in Observation No. 1 of this report.

Furthermore, the Municipal Accountant effected a fund transfer from Trust Fund to General Fund in April 2019 comprising of the unexpended balances of the 70% Mitigation Fund for CYs 2016 to 2018.   On the disbursements made in the Trust Fund in CY 2018 which already formed part of the total utilization accounted in CY 2017, the Municipal Accountant has already reverted back to the Trust Fund the amount P447,548.80 through a fund transfer made in November 2019.  The Municipal Accountant already disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements for CY 2019 the amounts and details of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF.





The BAC has addressed only few of the observations on the procurement undertaken in CY 2019.
observations.reason:
Not 
Implemented | The Municipal Accountant failed to set-up/transfer the unexpended balance of 30% QRF and 70% Mitigation Fund – MOOE for CY 2019 to the Special Trust Fund.  Based on the available documents, the Audit Team also observed that the information on the continuing projects presented in the LDRRMFIP, Status of DRRMF, Report on Utilization of DRRMF and disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements were incorrect and incomplete.   Furthermore, the breakdown of the transactions totaling P92,544.00 considered as Training Expenses in the Report on Utilization of DRRMF for CY 2017 was still not submitted to the Audit Team.  No explanations/
justifications were also provided by the Municipality on the source of fund for the disbursements in CY 2018 totaling P78,297.00 pertaining to the project – Fabrication of Collapsible Tents.    Comparison of the information contained in the Status of DRRMF and the Report on Utilization of DRRMF also disclosed a discrepancy in the total utilization amounting to P6,908.00.  Various deficiencies were likewise noted in the disclosure on the amounts and details of the unexpended balances of LDRRMF in the Notes to Financial Statements rendering the total unexpended balance of LDRRMF unreliable.  The Audit Team also observed that the unutilized balance of DRRMF for CY 2011 amounting to P108,000.00 was not yet reverted back to the Trust Fund – Proper bank account.


Reiterated in Observation No. 2 of this report.

staff of the Accounting Unit carefully prepare the reports pertaining to the appropriation and utilization of the projects charged against the 20% DF to be able to present reliable information to the users thereof.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

exert more effort for timely preparation and submission of the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements;

obtain the necessary documents to support the reconciling items identified and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balance of Cash, LCCA in the books of the Agency; and

maintain subsidiary ledgers of all bank accounts.
observations.managementAction: Up to this reporting, the bank reconciliation statements (BRSs) together with the bank statements and paid checks submitted by the Municipality were up to October 2019 only.   Verification thereof showed that the Municipal Accountant exerted efforts in the early submission of the same to the Audit Team.  The Municipal Accountant also ensured reconciliation of the total unadjusted book balances of the bank accounts maintained in the Trust Fund and the balance of the Cash in Bank - Local Currency, Current Account forwarded in the trial balance as of October 31, 2019.
observations.reason: Significant delay was observed by the Audit Team in the submission of BRSs by the Municipal Accountant which is mainly attributable to the delay incurred by the bank in furnishing the bank statements.  There were also reconciling items appearing in the BRSs for the month of October, majority of which are the interests earned, which were made known only to the Municipal Accountant upon receipt of the bank statements.  Delayed recording of the interest income in the books is also attributable to the aforementioned lapses.  Subsidiary ledgers for each bank account were also not submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management exert effort in determining if the National Solid Waste Management Commission approved their 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan.   Moreover, comply strictly with the provisions of Republic Act No. 9003.

The projects – Construction of Central MRF for 15 Barangays and Construction of Multi-Purpose Building for MRF Equipment/
Machineries (Phase I) were also implemented during the year.
observations.reason: Up to this reporting, no document was submitted to the Audit Team to prove that the 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan of the Municipality is conditionally approved by the National Solid Waste Management Commission.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

instruct the BAC to use the standard Philippine Bidding Documents, conduct all the mandatory stages required in the procurement process and observe the prescribed period of action on procurement activities; and

direct the TWG to strengthen the internal control on the bid evaluation and post-qualification process.

observations.reason: The BAC failed to fulfill substantial compliance on the audit recom-mendations as there were same observations noted in the review of contracts in CY 2019.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: 

instruct the BAC to use the standard bidding forms prescribed by the GPPB, conduct all the mandatory stages required in the procurement process and observe the prescribed period of action on procurement activities;

direct the TWG to observe the proper post-qualification process; and

instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of the requirements for all disbursements made.
observations.managementAction: The BAC implemented some of the audit recom-mendations.
observations.reason: There were still lapses noted in the review of the procurement activities of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the lacking documents aforementioned be submitted to the Audit Team and compliance to pertinent rules and regulations relative to disbursements for infrastructure projects including the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 be strictly implemented.   Also, we recommended that the Acceptance and Inspection Report be properly accomplished indicating the date received and inspected to be able to determine whether to impose penalties or not.
observations.managementAction: Some of the observations were already addressed in the payments made in CY 2019.
observations.reason: Audit of payments on infrastructure projects for CY 2019 disclosed that the Contractor’s Affidavit on payment of laborers and materials was still not attached in the progress payments.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the lacking documents aforementioned be submitted to the Audit Team and compliance to pertinent rules and regulations relative to disbursements for infrastructure projects including the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 be strictly implemented.  We also recommended that the Acceptance and Inspection Report be properly accomplished indicating the date received and inspected to be able to determine whether to impose penalties or not.
observations.managementAction: Some of the observations were already addressed in the payments made in CY 2019.
observations.reason: Audit of payments on infrastructure projects for CY 2019 disclosed that the Contractor’s Affidavit on payment of laborers and materials was still not attached in the progress payments.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management strictly follow Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 of the DILG and DBM as well as  its amendments under Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017  in the implementation of projects/programs/activities for the 20% Development Fund.
observations.managementAction: All the projects identified in the AIP for CY 2019 were in accordance with Item 3.0 of the JMC.  Also, majority of the PPAs charged against the 20% DF were implemented in CY 2019.  Furthermore, the Municipal Treasurer had already transferred the amount pertaining to the 20% DF to its own bank account in March 2019.
observations.reason:
The Audit Team still observed that the Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (MOOE) accounts were used in recording the disbursements of some projects. 

Reiterated in Observation No. 3 of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor:

require the Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of all Inventories and Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) accounts of the Municipality so that the corresponding report can be submitted to the Audit Team;  and

instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the resulting balance of the physical count with the accounting records in order to present the correct balances of said accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Management conducted physical count of the properties at year-end.
observations.reason:
Comparison made by the Audit Team between the RPCPPE and the accounting records revealed an unreconciled difference amounting to P55,846,610.78.  This showed that the Municipality still failed to conduct complete physical count of the properties at year-end.  

Reiterated in Observation No. 1 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to obtain from the bank and the Municipal Treasurer’s Office all the necessary documents needed to support the identified reconciling items and adjust Cash in Bank - LCCA account as appropriate.
observations.managementAction: Up to this reporting, the BRSs together with the bank statements and paid checks submitted by the Municipality were up to October 2019 only.  Verification thereof showed that the Municipal Accountant exerted efforts in the early submission of the same to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: Significant delay was observed by the Audit Team in the submission of BRSs by the Municipal Accountant which is mainly attributable to the delay incurred by the bank in furnishing the bank statements.   There were also reconciling items appearing in the BRSs for the month of October, majority of which are the interests earned, which were made known only to the Municipal Accountant upon receipt of the bank statements.  Delayed recording of the interest income in the books is also attributable to the aforementioned lapses.

observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer had already indicated the dates of actual receipt of remittances from the collectors in the Report of Collections and Deposits.
observations.reason: The missing ORs reported in CY 2015 were not yet submitted by the Office of the Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Accountant was advised to give priority to the timely submission of the accounts and financial reports within the reglementary period as required under COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009, COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995 and Section 70, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs to enable the Audit Team to conduct timely audit thereof and promptly inform the Management of the deficiencies noted, if any.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office exerted efforts in the early submission of the accounts, monthly trial balances and financial reports of the Municipality.
observations.reason: Minimal delay was observed by the Audit Team in the submission of the accounts and reports of the Municipality by the Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor of the agency, to see to it that disbursement vouchers are completely supported with valid documents before processing the claims.    Likewise, instruct also the BAC to comply strictly with the provisions of Section 52.1 of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor remind the BAC of its duties and responsibilities relative to purchases of goods through negotiated procurement - two-failed bidding.  Also remind the Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor, to adhere to the basic documentary requirements provided in COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor remind the BAC of its duties and responsibilities relative to purchases of goods through public bidding.  Submit lacking documents for the purchases of various goods to prove validity, regularity and propriety of subject transactions.
observations.managementAction: Some of the observations were already addressed in the procurements undertaken by the Municipality in CY 2019.
observations.reason: There are still lapses noted in the review of the procurement activities of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor, should see to it that all the necessary documentary requirements attached to the vouchers are accurate and complete before processing the claim.
observations.managementAction: Some of the documentary requirements were already complied with in the audit of payments for CY 2019.
observations.reason: The Audit Team still noted the absence of Contractor’s Affidavit on payment of laborers and materials in the progress payments for CY 2019.  No payments for consultancy projects were made in CY 2019 as there was no procurement of consulting services undertaken in that year.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the Contract of Agreement and its attachments within five (5) working days from the execution of the contract as required by Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We further recommended that the BAC submit the lacking documents cited above to the Audit Team to comply with the requirements of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 and address the deficiencies enumerated.
observations.managementAction: The BAC has addressed some of the observations on the procurement process undertaken in CY 2019.
observations.reason: Other observations were still noted in the review of contracts in CY 2019.

observations.managementAction: No information can be found in the results of the review by the DILG that the PPAs reflected in the GPB constitute general issues.   This only implied that all the PPAs incorporated therein addressed gender issues.
observations.reason:
Given the adjustments made in the budget allocated to some programs, the Municipality failed to meet the 5% minimum budgetary requirement for GAD Fund.

Reiterated in Observation No. 4 of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the proper appropriation of five percent (5%) of the estimated revenue from regular sources as its LDRRM Fund to support disaster risk management.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the submission of the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF using the format in Annex B to the Audit Team, in compliance with Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.  Likewise, submit the recommendation from the LDRRMO of the Municipality to transfer said funds to other LDRRMCs and declaration of state of calamity in support to the above mentioned disbursements.   Instruct the Municipal Accountant to properly classify each transaction so that it will be recorded in their proper accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to adhere strictly with the accounting and reporting guidelines set forth by COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.  Maintain also a separate subsidiary ledger for DRRM activities in the Trust Fund books.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2019, the Municipality has properly appropriated 5% of its revenue from regular sources to support its disaster risk and management activities.  The Municipal Accountant also submitted the Report on Utilization of LDRRMF for CY 2019 to the Audit Team.  The amounts and details of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF was already disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements for CY 2019.
observations.reason:
The Municipal Accountant failed to set-up/transfer the unexpended balance of 30% QRF and 70% Mitigation Fund – MOOE for CY 2019 to the Special Trust Fund.  Various deficiencies were also noted in the disclosure on the amounts and details of the unexpended balances of LDRRMF in the Notes to Financial Statements rendering the total unexpended balance of LDRRMF unreliable.    

Reiterated in Observation No. 2 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor along with all the concerned officials and employees strictly follow the provisions of DILG-DBM in planning the programs/projects/activities funded by 20% Development Fund and their implementation.  See to it also that those development projects identified in the Annual Investment Plan were fully implemented for the benefit of the constituents.
observations.managementAction: All the projects identified in the AIP for CY 2019 were in accordance with Item 3.0 of the JMC.  The Municipality see to it that majority of the PPAs charged against the 20% DF were implemented in CY 2019.
observations.reason:
The Audit Team still observed that the MOOE accounts were used in recording the disbursements of some projects. 

Reiterated in Observation No. 3 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management direct the Inventory Committee to conduct complete physical count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE); and the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile the accounting and treasury property records and immediately submit the Report of the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.managementAction: The LCE issued an order to the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of PPE. On-going reconciliation between the treasury and accounting department.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer furnish the Accounting Office with a certified list of taxpayers with the stated amount due and collectible for the year to enable to set-up the RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of each year and make necessary adjusting entries.
observations.managementAction: The management adopted the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to document the request for the write-off of the dormant accounts and strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, to ensure the fair presentation of the account balances in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Demand letters were prepared and issued by management. Requests for write-off of dormant accounts have already been facilitated and for submission to COA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management prepare a 5-Year Business Plan for every Local Economic Enterprises (LEE) to provide a clear direction of the operation aimed towards financial viability and sustainability.
observations.managementAction: The LCE already issued a Memo designating technical working groups to focus on the drafting of business plan for the LEE of LGU San Agustin. The said team is now in the initial phase of gathering needed data and documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management follow-up its application for land titling to ensure that all lands owned by the LGU are registered under the Torrens Title System in pursuant to the provisions of Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386.
observations.managementAction: The management acted on the titling of lands and still pending for approval. Frequent follow-ups have been made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management employ measure to ensure compliance in the timely submission of the Sources and Utilization of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund as required under Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012 – 002. Moreover, we recommend that the LDRRM Officer coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to prepare the said report on a monthly basis.
We recommended that the Management ensure the completeness of documentary requirements before processing the fund transfers pursuant to the provision of Section 4(6) of P.D. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The management implemented the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System adhere to the provisions of Section 3.0 of Section 3.0 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular 2016-01 and facilitate the conduct of gender analysis using the HGDG tool for gender-responsive PPAs through its GAD Focal Point System.

Instruct the HRMO to require the JO/COS to submit Tax Identification Number (TIN) and Annual Registration Fee (ARF) upon hiring and renewal of contracts, respectively; Require the Municipal Accountant to compute, withhold and remit the respective percentage taxes of JO/COS Personnel for CY 2018, as provided in RMC 51-2018 and other pertinent laws, rules and regulations; Capacitate the Municipal Accounting Personnel, by requiring them to attend seminars and trainings related to the latest updates on Tax and BIR Rulings.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee be directed to finish the conduct of physical count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), reconcile the Accounting and Treasury property records and immediately submit the Report of the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.managementAction: The LCE issued an order to the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of PPE. On-going reconciliation between the treasury and accounting department.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management employ measure to ensure compliance in the timely submission of the Sources and Utilization of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund as required under Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012 – 002. Moreover, we recommend that the LDRRM Officer coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to prepare the said report on monthly basis.

We recommended that Management prepare a 5-Year Business Plan for every Local Economic Enterprises (LEE) to provide a clear direction of the operation aimed towards financial viability and sustainability.
observations.managementAction: The LCE already issued a Memo designating technical working groups to focus on the drafting of business plan for the LEE of LGU San Agustin. The said team is now in the initial phase of gathering needed data and documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the guidelines set under Sections 37 of Republic Act No. 9003 in order to deliver to its constituents the maximum benefits of which this Act is created.

We recommended that Management adhere to the provisions of Section 3.0 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2016-01 by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA and facilitate the conduct of gender analysis using the HGDG tool for proper identification of gender-responsive PPAs through its GAD Focal Point System.

We recommended Management to direct the Municipal Accountant to document the request for write-off of the dormant accounts and strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular 2016-005 to ensure fair presentation of the account balances in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Demand letters were prepared and issued by management. Requests for write-off of dormant accounts have already been facilitated and for submission to COA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management include in the GPB appropriations for the attendance of its officials and employees to GAD trainings that will deepen their understanding of the GAD Program and their roles on the mainstreaming of the said program into local policy-making, planning, programming, budgeting, implementation, monitoring and evaluation. In case the LGU opts to attribute a portion or a whole of its budget to the GAD Budget, we also recommended that Management conduct a training program on the use of the HGDG in its GPB to assure the proper determination of the amounts that may be attributed to the GAD Budget.

For the deficiency noted, we recommended that the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board: Prepare the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan containing, but not limited to, the components listed in Section 17 of Republic Act No. 9003 in coordination with the various sectors within the locality; Conduct regular monitoring on the implementation of the Solid Waste Management Program for the early resolution of the problems, issues and concerns encountered during its implementation; and Conduct information and educational campaign at the barangay level with emphasis on their responsibility on solid waste segregation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor employ measure to ensure compliance of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office in the submission of the Sources and Utilization of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund as required under Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012 – 002.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee be directed to finish the conduct of physical count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), reconcile the Accounting and Treasury property records and immediately submit the Report of the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.managementAction: The LCE issued an order to the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of PPE. On-going reconciliation between the treasury and accounting department

Disregard the irrelevant accounting policies cited in paragraph 3.4 above so as not to misled users on the understanding of the agency’s consolidated financial statements.

accurate and reliable financial information.

Pay directly the suppliers for the purchase of materials, supplies and other operating expenses;
Limit payments through reimbursement and deduct/withhold the mandatory taxes; and
 Strictly comply with provisions of Section 93 of PD 1445 and Section 2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997

Exert effort for the immediate settlement of the above NS and ND; and
Ensure that all employees who are retiring or transferring to other agencies shall first settle the suspensions and disallowances for which they are liable, otherwise, the officials who issued their clearances shall be jointly liable for the unsettled disallowances of the retired employees.

observations.reason: Enforcement of full and immediate settlement of ND was not made by the LGU.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the agency should reflect in the agency books the ₱9,310,397.86 as adjustments in the affected accounts. After adjustments, the books and the financial statements should conform to the adjusted cash balance per bank reconciliation as of December 31, 2018.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to evaluate and determine without delay the transactions which caused the variance and effect reconciliation thereof and to perform the same regularly, pursuant to Section 181 (c) of the GAAM, Volume I, to present fairly the cash balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management:
Require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to cause the field collecting officer to restitute the remaining balance of P169,000.00 immediately and relieve him of his duties as collecting officer.
Sanctions against erring accountable officer must be imposed as provided under Article 217, Revised Penal Code which provides that “The failure of the accountable officer to have duly forthcoming any public funds or property with which he is chargeable, upon demand by a duly authorized officer, shall be a prima facie evidence that he has put such missing funds or property to personal use.
Require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to personally monitor the receipt and issuance of accountable forms to designated collecting officers through properly accomplished DSCAF/RAAF.

We reiterated our recommendation that management require the Municipal Accountant to:
Issue demand letters to notify the concerned officials and employees of their responsibility to settle their unliquidated cash advances. If no settlement was made, withhold from their salaries considering that they have been notified and given adequate time to liquidate.
Issue demand letters to employees who already resigned or transferred to other offices for the immediate liquidation or settlement of the unliquidated balances
Request for the write-off of the unliquidated balance of cash advances of the deceased employees, when applicable.
Reclassify “Other Receivables” account to Advances to Officers and Employees account in order to present fairly the total balance of unsettled cash advances.
Ensure the completeness of prescribed supporting documents for the granting of cash advances before processing claims for payment thereof.
Not grant additional cash advance to concerned officials and employees unless the previous cash advance granted to them have been liquidated.
Strictly monitor periodically the submission of liquidation reports to ensure timely accounting thereof to avoid undue accumulation of outstanding cash advances.
Strictly comply with the provisions of the abovementioned Circulars.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to identify the cause or reason of the differences and make the necessary adjusting entries, if any, to be able to present fairly the balance of Advances to Officers and Employees account in the Financial Statement.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Locate all available documents regarding the abovementioned accounts in order to prepare the necessary subsidiary ledgers.
Exhaust all possible remedies to locate records or documents of the balances of long outstanding receivables in order to require the concerned agencies/organizations to immediately cause the liquidation and settlement of the same and/or request for the write-off of dormant accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accounting Office record all purchases of supplies and materials for stocks as an inventory and adjust the account for whatever consumption on the basis of Summary of Supplies and Material Issued (SSMI), duly supported with the original copy of Requisition Issue Slip (RIS), which should be prepared and submitted weekly or monthly by the GSO/supply Officer to the OIC-Municipal Accountant.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management:
Prioritize the reconciliation of balances between the accounting records and RPCPPE. Prepare adjusting entries, if any, to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts.
Should consider to dispose the unserviceable PPEs, after reconciliation was made, and derecognize such in the Agency’s book of accounts.
Maintain a detailed Equipment Ledger Card for each class of Property, Plant and Equipment that supports the balances presented in the FS. The schedule should enable the user to trace for the acquisition date and cost of an individual PPE to facilitate depreciation computation.

We recommended that management:
require the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the implementing department (e.g. Engineering Office) to identify, determine and reconcile the projects for proper classification as to whether completed or in progress.
require the Engineering Office to provide the Accounting Office with copies of a comprehensive report of completed projects and other necessary documents; and
based on the adjusted data, require the Municipal Accountant to adjust the balances of the affected accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Direct the above-mentioned key officials to accomplish the duties and responsibilities assigned to them; and
Ensure compliance with accounting and reporting guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace the period or date of acquisition of the PPEs mentioned above and compute for the depreciation based on the estimated useful life of assets as provided under COA Circular 2003-007. Prior Years’ depreciation should be taken up as Prior Period Adjustment to be closed to Government Equity and the current year’s depreciation should be recorded as expense.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Assess the nature of the biological assets held by the Municipality. Thereafter, adopt an accounting policy that best suits the Municipality’s biological assets.
Perform physical count on the biological assets.
Properly include in the Notes to Financial Statements the required disclosures as per IPSAS 27.

We recommended that the Accounting Office prepare the necessary adjusting entries to properly recognize the Due to GOCCs and Due to LGUs account. Henceforth, record receipts and disbursements of fund transfers under the specific account as prescribed under the Revised Chart of Accounts for the proper monitoring of transactions relating thereon.
We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant revisit the entries made and make the necessary adjustment to record the proper account.
We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant revisit the transactions recorded as LDRRM, and make the necessary adjustment to derecognize the amount and record the proper account.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepares financial statements to include and provide the requirements called for under the aforementioned circular.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Locate all available documents regarding the abovementioned accounts in order to prepare the necessary subsidiary ledgers.
Exhaust all possible remedies to reconcile the balances of those payable account in which SL is maintained. Prepare necessary adjusting entries, if any.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Revisit the entry made on Due from Other Funds in CY 2017 amounting to P3,668,601.49 and explain why no counterpart entry on Due to Other Funds was made.
Prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and take up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are adjusted on timely basis.
Perform constant review of transactions to deter errors and omissions.
We recommended the Municipal Accountant to revisit the entries made and make the necessary adjustment to record the proper account

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain an extensive record for all its accounts by maintaining a subsidiary ledger for each class of a specific account. For “Repairs and Maintenance”, use the sub-ledger accounts identified in the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units.
Also, we recommended that the agency consider the proper classification of disbursements charged under the Repairs and Maintenance – Infrastructure Assets account for fair presentation of financial statements.

We recommended that management submit the complete list of the beneficiaries of the financial assistance including their addresses. Further, submit explanation or provide the Audit Team with the parameters in choosing the qualified victims as well as the basis for the variation of amount of financial assistance granted.

We recommended that the Municipality open a separate depository account with the Local Branch of the Land Bank of the Philippines (LBP) for the Supplemental Feeding and Senior Citizen Social Pension Programs as required in the Omnibus Guidelines in the Implementation of the Supplemental Feeding Program.

We recommended that the Municipality utilize its Local Government Support Fund – Financial Assistance as balanced as possible in accordance with the provisions of DBM Local Budget Circular No. 117 in order to maximize the purpose and intended benefits of the fund.
Also, we recommended that the LGU device an effective and efficient way of determining beneficiaries of the LGSF – FA in order to maximize the purpose of the fund.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly adhere to the provisions of DBM Local Budget Circular No. 117 on the utilization and implementation of the Local Government Support Fund – Financial Assistance for transparency and accountability.
We recommended that the agency strictly comply with proper accounting for receipts and utilization of trust funds, recognizing only those that merit the scope of trust funds under the account.

We recommended that the agency submit the required supporting documents for the abovementioned expenditures to avoid suspension in audit of the said disbursements.
Also, in the future, in order to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant not to process claims of payment unless all the required supporting documents are present and attached to the disbursement vouchers.

We recommended that management ensure that PAPs funded out of the 20% DF are implemented within the period specified in the AIP. For PAPs that are still unimplemented and on-going, positive efforts should be made to fast track the completion of the said PAPs. In addition, submit explanation on the delay of completion of the PAPs most especially those under the CYs 2016-2017.

We recommended that Management:
Allocate the 20% development fund for projects that shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures;
Ensure that the utilization of Development Fund must be for capital expenditure and/or in the nature of investments; and
Strict adherence with the DILG and DBM joint Circular No. 2017-1 in the preparation of Annual Investment Plan to be funded out of the 20% EDF to ensure that all items to be funded are development programs/projects and activities which are compliant with the guidelines.

We recommended that the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer submit the required reports on the sources and utilization of DRRMF to COA on or before the 15th day after the end of each month through the LDRRMC and Local Development Council in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the Municipality fully and timely implement its programs/projects/activities to ensure that preparedness, mitigation and rehabilitation projects could be achieved in order to avoid, lessen or limit the potential adverse impact of disasters. Also the LDRRMC should regularly monitor and evaluate its implementation.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant maintain complete subsidiary ledgers for each special account in the general fund and prepare the corresponding financial reports to form part of the schedules of the general fund trial balance as required under the abovementioned guidelines. This will serve as a tool in informing management on the condition as well as the status of operation of these special accounts.

We recommended that the Management:
Submit justification on the necessity of hiring 242 job order employees, even though there are duplication of work.
Submit justification on allowing some of the JO employees to render services during Saturdays, Sundays and holidays when in fact their work performed are routinary and can be performed during ordinary days.
Ensure that hiring of job order employees are based on necessity of the Office/Department to avoid waste of government funds
Prepare a Contract of Service between the LGU and the JO employees to inform the latter of his/her duties and responsibilities. Also, the Contract of Service can also be a tool by management to verify if there is duplication of work among the JO employees.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Enforce the full settlement of the above-listed disallowances by the persons liable; and
Ensure compliance with COA rules on settlement of accounts.

We recommended that the Management see to it that the amount allocated are disbursed for strengthening and implementing the programs of LCPC. Moreover, prioritize programs for the children in conflict with the law and those who are at risk.
Also, we recommended the management to come up with Local Development Plan containing the strategic plan of the LCPC to specify specific projects, programs, and activities consistent with DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 addressing the issues concerning the children who are in conflict with the law and those who are at risk.

We recommended that management prepare the LGU’s GAD Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Reports using the prescribed format required in PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2016-01 dated January 12, 2016. Also, submit the said reports to the Auditor within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the municipality utilize the GAD Budget in accordance with PCW-DBM-DILG-NEDA-Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 and refrain from charging to the GAD Fund the aforementioned not GAD related activities in order to attain the purpose for which the fund was created. Submit also justification why these charges were disbursed out of GAD Fund.

We recommended that the Management implement CSC RO2 Decision No. 17-0062 dated March 28, 2017. As a result of non-implementation the disbursement vouchers certified by the OIC-Municipal Accountant may be considered invalid and could be disallowed in audit. This matter will be referred to COA Legal Office for further clarification as whether the disbursements signed by her during the period she acted as OIC-Municipal Accountant is valid and lawful.
Further, we recommended that management observe the CSC guidelines on the designation of Officer-in-Charge of an Office.

We recommended that Management, through its Inventory
Committee, conduct complete physical count of the properties of the
Municipality, reconcile the results of the count with the accounting records
and submit the complete and accurate RPCI and RPCPPE to the Audit
Team, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs,
Volume 1.

67

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a) LDRRM Council to prepare the LDRRMFIP of the Municipality fully
addressing the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002;
b) Municipal Accountant to ensure strict compliance with the accounting
and reporting guidelines set forth under the Circular in order to correctly
present the status of LDRRMF at year-end;
c) Municipal Accountant to furnish the Audit Team with the breakdown of
the amount considered as expenses in CY 2017 for the activity – Training
Expenses;
d) Municipal Accountant to revert back to the Trust Fund the amount
disbursed for the expenses which were already charged against the 5%
Calamity Fund for CY 2017;
e) Municipal Accountant to carefully prepare the Schedule of Trust
Liabilities – DRRMF to establish reliability thereof;
f) concerned personnel to effect a fund transfer from the Calamity Fund
bank account to Trust Fund – Proper bank account pertaining to the
unutilized balance of DRRMF for CY 2011 which was transferred to the
General Fund on December 2016; and
g) concerned personnel to properly coordinate in the preparation of
various reports for consistency of information.
The Audit Team also verified the compliance of the BAC with the
provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 in the procurement
activities undertaken for the projects charged against the 5% Calamity Fund and
observed that:
The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) committed procedural lapses in the
procurement activities undertaken for the projects charged against the 5%
Calamity Fund which were not in accordance with the provisions of the 2016
Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of Republic Act (R.A.)
No. 9184, thus affecting the propriety of the Purchase Orders (POs).
88
In terms of contract cost, the most significant projects subjected to review
by the Audit Team are as follows:
Name of Project Contract Cost
Purchase of Materials for the Repair and Improvement of P 587,555.00
6 CL 1 Storey School Building at San Agustin Central
Elementary School (labeled as PO No. 1)
Purchase of Materials for the Construction of Line Canal 292,015.00
and Storm Drainage (labeled as PO No. 2)
Purchase of Food Supplies – Buffer Stock (labeled as PO 199,899.80
No. 3)
Review of the projects disclosed various deficiencies. The following are
the common observations noted in the three POs:
 The General and Special Conditions of Contract which formed part of the
bidding documents were noted to be lacking.
 Conflicting information was observed in the contents of the Invitation to Bid
(ITB) and the Instruction to Bidders Clause 12.1 (a) (ii) of the Bid Data Sheet.
It is stated in the former that bidders should have completed within 30
calendar days from the date of submission and receipt of bids a contract
similar to the project. On the other hand, it is required in the latter document
that the bidder’s Single Largest Completed Contract (SLCC) similar to the
contract to be bid should have been completed within the last two years prior
to the deadline for the submission and receipt of bids. This is common to PO
Nos. 1 and 2.
 The proof of receipt of the invitation letter mailed to the representative of the
Philippine Chamber of Commerce and Industry was not provided. Thus,
compliance to Section 13.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 is
difficult to determine. This is common to PO Nos. 1 and 2.
 Information on the opening of sealed envelopes during the bid opening was
not provided in the Minutes of Bid Opening.
The Municipal Engineer noted the observation.

with Section 96 (a) and (b) of R.A. No. 7160 everytime he will travel outside
the province.
Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant properly
check the claims submitted to the Accounting Office in order to arrive at the
amount of travelling expenses which the itinerant is entitled to.

1.6	We recommended that the Management direct the Inventory Committee to conduct complete physical count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE); and the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile the accounting and treasury property records and immediately submit the Report of the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) to the Office of the Auditor.

2.6	We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer furnish the Accounting Office with a certified list of taxpayers with the stated amount due and collectible for the year to enable to set-up the RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of each year and make necessary adjusting entries.

3.6	We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to document the request for the write-off of the dormant accounts and strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, to ensure the fair presentation of the account balances in the financial statements.

4.5	We recommended that the Management prepare a 5-Year Business Plan for every Local Economic Enterprises (LEE) to provide a clear direction of the operation aimed towards financial viability and sustainability.

5.6	We recommended that the Management follow-up its application for land titling to ensure that all lands owned by the LGU are registered under the Torrens Title System in pursuant to the provisions of Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386.

6.5	We recommended that the Management employ measure to ensure compliance in the timely submission of the Sources and Utilization of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund as required under Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012 – 002. Moreover, we recommend that the LDRRM Officer coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to prepare the said report on a monthly basis.
7.5	We recommended that the Management ensure the completeness of documentary requirements before processing the fund transfers pursuant to the provision of Section 4(6) of P.D. 1445.

8.6	We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare subsidiary ledgers for each trust liability and to reconcile the same to the reported amount on the book of accounts thereby fairly presenting the balances to the financial statement and to facilitate the reversion of the unexpended balance of the completed projects to the National Treasury for utilization to other developmental projects.

9.5	We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System adhere to the provisions of Section 3.0 of Section 3.0 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular 2016-01 and facilitate the conduct of gender analysis using the HGDG tool for gender-responsive PPAs through its GAD Focal Point System.

11.7	We recommended that the Municipal Engineer fast-track the preparation of the program of works and the Management to ensure the full implementation of its priority programs and projects that are envisioned to help in the attainment of socio-economic development and environmental outcomes.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Correct thoroughly the irregularities in the balances of the accounts noted in order for the amounts in the financial statements, general ledger and subsidiary ledger be parallel with each other; and
Consider a shift from manual accounting system to computerized accounting system which offers various advantages as compared to manual.

We recommended that Management:
Consider the accounting policies of the accounts noted above as an addition to its summary of significant accounting policies for a better understanding of the agency’s financial statements. Furthermore, the agency should refer to the provisions of PPSAS 27 (Biological Assets) as regards to the required disclosures; and
Disregard the irrelevant accounting policies cited in paragraph 3.4 above so as not to misled users on the understanding of the agency’s consolidated financial statements.

We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
Reflect in the agency books the amount of P2,719,068.34 as adjustments in the affected accounts. After adjustments, the books and the financial statements should conform to the adjusted cash balance per bank reconciliation as of December 31, 2017.
Account for the differences and effect adjustment/s, if necessary and general ledger must be filled out correctly and updated regularly.

We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
Locate all available documents regarding the abovementioned accounts in order to prepare the necessary subsidiary ledgers; and
Exhaust all possible remedies to locate records or documents of the balances of long outstanding balances in order to require the concerned agencies/organizations to immediately cause the liquidation and settlement of the receivable accounts, otherwise, request for the write-off of dormant accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated 
December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office record all purchases to its corresponding inventory account classification and to adjust the account for whatever consumption on the basis of Summary of Supplies and Material Issued (SSMI), duly supported with the original copy of Requisition Issue Slip (RIS), which should be prepared and submitted weekly or monthly to the OIC-Municipal Accountant.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and take up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are adjusted on timely basis.
Perform constant review of transactions to deter error and omissions.
We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to reclassify the Market and Slaughterhouse operation of the agency as investment property.
We recommended that Management submit to the Audit Team the documentary requirements enumerated under item 13.1 of COA Circular 2012-01 for audit purposes.

We recommended that Management:
Prioritize the reconciliation of balances between the accounting records and RPCPPE. Prepare adjusting entries, if any, to reflect the correct balance of the affected accounts;
Should consider to dispose the unserviceable PPE, after reconciliation is made, and derecognize such in the Agency’s book of accounts; and
Maintain a detailed Equipment Ledger Card for each class of Property, Plant and Equipment that supports the balances presented in the FS. The schedule should enable the user to trace for the acquisition date and cost of an individual PPE to facilitate depreciation computation.

We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
Assess the nature of the biological assets held by the Municipality. Thereafter, adopt an accounting policy that best suits the Municipality’s biological assets;
Perform physical count of the biological assets; and
Properly include in the Notes to Financial Statements the required disclosures as per PPSAS 27.

In the exit conference, Management committed to locate the documents pertaining to the biological assets and a physical inventory will also be performed to verify existence.

We recommended that Management instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
Locate all available documents regarding the abovementioned accounts in order to prepare the necessary subsidiary ledgers.
Exhaust all possible remedies to reconcile the balances of those payable account in which SL is maintained. Prepare necessary adjusting entries, if any.

We recommended that the OIC- Municipal Accountant to:
Maintain special accounts in the general fund; and
Prepare the corresponding financial reports to form part of the schedules of the general fund. This will serve as a tool in informing management on the condition as well as the status of operation of these special accounts.

We recommended that Management instruct:
The OIC-Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments to transfer the unutilized balance of LDRRMF to Trust Liability –DRRMF account under the Trust Fund account; and
The Municipal Treasurer to deposit the unutilized fund to the LDRRMF bank account.
We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant to revisit the transactions recorded as LDDRM, and make the necessary adjustment to derecognize the amount and record the proper account.

We recommended that the municipal government implement fully the planned programs, activities and projects for disaster preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery so that the DRRM Funds would be maximized and the intended benefits would be fully achieved.

We recommended that Management:
Explain why the POW includes the cost of gasoline only as well as the non-inclusion of materials and labor cost;
Submit the following documents for audit purposes:
Approved POW  (for Infrastructure Projects) showing the budgeted cost of P5,000,000.00
Statement of Work Accomplished
Pictures showing before, during and after constructions of the local roads for each barangay;
Inspection Report by the Agency’s Authorized Engineer;
Certificate of Project Completion; and
Certificate of Acceptance by the different concerned Punong Barangays.
Strict adherence with the DILG and DBM joint Circular No. 2017-1 in the preparation of Annual Investment Plan to be funded out of the 20% EDF; and
Ensure that the utilization of 20% Development Fund must be for capital expenditure and/or in the nature of investments.

We recommended that Management:
Explain why the procurement of gasoline for Construction/ Rehabilitation/ Improvement of Local Roads (23 Barangays) Project were made in piecemeal or in series although the same can be conducted in a single procurement activity thru public bidding.
Refrain from resorting to other alternative methods of procurement of goods by splitting the purchase order and/or contracts.
Strictly comply with the provisions set forth in the R.A. 9184 and its Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations.

We recommended that management facilitate the immediate implementation of the infrastructure projects funded from the 20% Development Fund so that the desired services and benefits of these PPAs be delivered to the constituents of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management comply strictly to Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 and Section 9.1 of COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and submit the perfected contracts with all the required supporting documents as basis for timely and objective auditorial, legal and technical review.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant not to process claims of  progress billing payment unless  all the required supporting documents are attached to the disbursement vouchers; and
Comply strictly to Section 9.1.1.1 to 9.1.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated  June 14, 2012.

We recommended that Management:
Refrain from resorting to other alternative methods of procurement of goods by splitting the purchase order and/or contracts.
Strictly comply with the provisions set forth in the R.A. 9184 and its Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations.

We recommended that Management:
Pay directly the suppliers for the purchase of materials, supplies and other operating expenses;
Limit payments through reimbursement and deduct/withhold the mandatory taxes; and
Strictly comply with provisions of Section 93 of PD 1445 and Section 2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997
We recommended that Management strictly comply with the above mentioned BIR Revenue Memorandum Circular.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustment in the recoding of the settlement and ND that becomes final and executory to be able to present the correct balance of the account Receivable/ Disallowance/Charges;
Exert effort for the immediate settlement of the above NS and ND; and
Ensure that all employees who are retiring or transferring to other agencies shall first settle the suspensions and disallowances for which they are liable, otherwise, the officials who issued their clearances shall be jointly liable for the unsettled disallowances of the retired employees.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through its Inventory Committee, conduct complete physical count of the properties of the Municipality, reconcile the results of the count with the accounting records and submit the complete and accurate RPCI and RPCPPE to the Audit Team, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Vice-Mayor limit the participants in the events conducted by their respective leagues.  Also, we recommended that he allow only the members of the committee concerned with the subject of the conference.

We further recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer must see to it that the claims of the officials are within the available funds of their department.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to remit to GSIS the withheld contributions and loan amortizations intact and within the prescribed period in compliance with Sections 6 and 7 of R.A. No. 8291.

Pag-IBIG (Pagtutulungan sa kinabukasan: Ikaw, Bangko, Industriya at Gobyerno)
Scrutiny of the documents (payrolls, vouchers, and the corresponding entries) with respect to the withholding and remittance of contributions and loan amortizations revealed some lapses as enumerated below:
The balance of Due to Pag-IBIG account as of December 31, 2017 amounted to P136,071.50.  Of this amount, P400.00 pertains to the Pag-IBIG contributions withheld on the payment of last salary of Ms. Florentina T. Galang on March 24, 2017.  This indicates that there were instances when remittance was not made within the period prescribed by the law.
Section 23 entitled Remittance of Contributions of R.A. No. 9769, otherwise known as Home Development Mutual Fund Law of 2009 provides the following:
“a) It shall be the duty of every employer, private or public, to set aside and remit the contributions required under this Act in accordance with a mechanism determined by the Board of Trustees.
b) Every employer required to set aside and remit such contributions as prescribed under this Act shall be liable for their payment, and nonpayment shall further subject the employer to a penalty of three percent (3%) per month of the amounts payable from the date the contributions fall due until paid.
c) It shall be mandatory and compulsory for all government instrumentalities, agencies, including government-owned and -controlled corporations, to provide the payment of contributions in their annual appropriations.  Penal sanctions shall be imposed upon these employers who fail to include the payment of contributions on time, or delay the remittance of the required contributions to the Fund.  The heads of offices and agencies shall be administratively liable for non-remittance of the required contributions to the Fund.
X x x”
The Municipal Accountant cited same reason.  She said that there were instances when the withheld contributions were not yet due since the records of the remittance agency were not yet updated.
The contributions and loan amortizations withheld on December totaling P133,913.86 which formed part of the ending balance of the Due to Pag-IBIG account were remitted on January 23, 2018 which is within the prescribed period.
It was also observed that Pag-IBIG contributions totaling P400.00 was withheld on the payment of first salary of Ms. Mabel Ann Yap Moreno-Correa on October 3, 2017.  This is recorded by crediting the Due to Pag-IBIG account.  However, the said account was then debited on December 28, 2017 by the same amount.  It was stated in the disbursement voucher that the transaction pertains to unremitted Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth contributions.  The entry made for the said transaction is presented below:
Dr. Due to Pag-IBIG				       400.00
Dr. Due to PhilHealth				                    	       500.00
Cr. Cash in Bank – Local Currency, Current Account	        450.00
Cr. Pag-IBIG Contributions				                                200.00
Cr. PhilHealth Contributions				                    250.00
It can be gleaned in the above entry that the Due to Pag-IBIG account was debited in the amount of P400.00.  The Pag-IBIG Contributions, an expense account, represents the government share in the contributions.  The entry made only showed that the Municipality did not withhold Pag-IBIG contributions (both personal and government share) on her salary.
The Municipal Accountant reasoned out that they can only remit what is billed to them.  In this case, the records of the Pag-IBIG were not yet updated, thus, they cannot remit the contributions withheld on the first salary of Ms. Mabel Ann Yap Moreno-Correa.  Once the records was updated, that is the only time they can remit the contributions of the said personnel.  Its effect was prospective, not retrospective.
Verification of the supporting documents for the remittance revealed that the OR issued by the Pag-IBIG acknowledging the remittance of the contributions and loan amortizations withheld on March was not attached in the disbursement voucher.  In lieu of the OR, the check number and its date were used as reference.
This is similar to the observation discussed in GSIS.  The same comment was given by the Municipal Accountant.
Delayed remittance of the amounts due to Pag-IBIG will result in the imposition of penalties payable by the Municipality.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to remit the withheld Pag-IBIG contributions and loan amortizations intact and within the prescribed period.

PHILHEALTH
Scrutiny of the documents (payrolls, vouchers, and the corresponding entries) with respect to the withholding and remittance of contributions revealed some lapses as enumerated below:
The balance of Due to PhilHealth account as of December 31, 2017 amounted to P40,775.00.  Of this amount, P200.00 pertains to the contributions withheld on the payment of last salary of Ms. Florentina T. Galang on March 24, 2017.  The ending balance is also inclusive of the contributions withheld totaling P1,250.00 on the salary differential of Mr. Neil T. Faigao which was paid on May 9, 2017.  The contributions withheld on the payment of final salary of Mr. Renato M. Manlunas totaling P225.00 also formed part of the ending balance.  Failure to timely remit the appropriate premium contributions constitute the imposition of the applicable interest and penalties as provided in Section 11 of R.A. No. 7875, otherwise known as The National Health Insurance Act of 2013.
The said provision states that the remittance of contribution shall be mandatory for all members.  It shall be made to PhilHealth offices or to any of the accredited collecting agents.  Failure to timely remit the appropriate premium contribution shall be subject to interest and penalties as prescribed by the Corporation without prejudice to other applicable penalties herein provided.
The Municipal Accountant cited same reason.  She said that there were instances when the withheld contributions were not yet due since the records of the remittance agency were not yet updated.
The contributions withheld on December totaling P37,050.00 which formed part of the ending balance of the Due to Pag-IBIG account were remitted on January 10, 2018 which is within the prescribed period.
There were two instances when the total amount withheld is not equal to the amount remitted thereof, as shown below:
The balance of P600.00 is still unremitted as of December 31, 2017.
It was also observed that PhilHealth contributions totaling P500.00 was withheld on the payment of first salary of Ms. Mabel Ann Yap Moreno-Correa on October 3, 2017.  This is recorded by crediting the Due to Philhealth account.  However, the said account was then debited on December 28, 2017 by the same amount.  It was stated in the disbursement voucher that the transaction pertains to unremitted Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth contributions.  The entry made for the said transaction is presented below:
Dr. Due to Pag-IBIG				       400.00
Dr. Due to PhilHealth				                    	       500.00
Cr. Cash in Bank – Local Currency, Current Account	        450.00
Cr. Pag-IBIG Contributions				                                200.00
Cr. PhilHealth Contributions				                    250.00
It can be gleaned in the above entry that the Due to PhilHealth account was debited in the amount of P500.00.  The PhilHealth Contributions, an expense account, represents the government share in the contributions.  The entry made only showed that the Municipality did not withhold PhilHealth contributions (both personal and government share) on her salary.
This observation was previously discussed in Pag-IBIG.  The same comment was given by the Municipal Accountant.
There were four instances of refund of PhilHealth contributions wherein the Due to PhilHealth account is debited in the total amount of P4,025.00 representing the personal and government shares in the contributions.  The entry made on January 2017 on the refund of contributions of Mr. Henry L. Galvan, Mr. Rowel R. Manasan and Mr. Mar Dominic A. Correa, compounded by the Audit Team, was presented below:
Dr. Due to PhilHealth				                  	    3,150.00
Cr. Cash in Bank – Local Currency, Current Account                 1,575.00
Cr. Prior Period Adjustment					                    1,575.00
Below was the entry made on July 2017 as regards the refund of Mr. Neil T. Faigao’s contributions:
Dr. Due to PhilHealth 				                    	        875.00
Cr. Cash in Bank – Local Currency, Current Account                   437.50
Cr. PhilHealth Contributions				                      437.50
The amount credited to Prior Period Adjustment and PhilHealth Contributions accounts represent the government share.  From the entries made, it can be inferred that the withheld government shares were not remitted.
Same comment was also given by the Municipal Accountant.
Verification of the supporting documents for the remittance revealed that the OR issued by the PhilHealth acknowledging the remittance of the contributions and loan amortizations withheld on February was not attached in the disbursement voucher.  In lieu of the OR, the check number and its date were used as reference.
This is similar to the observation discussed in GSIS.  The same comment was given by the Municipal Accountant.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to remit to PhilHealth the withheld contributions intact and within the prescribed period.

In view of the foregoing audit observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (LDRRMC) to comply strictly with the provisions of R.A. No. 10121 and COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.  Also, allow only those expenditures related to disaster risk management activities to be charged against the 5% Calamity Fund.
Municipal Accountant to adhere strictly to the accounting and reporting guidelines set forth under COA Circular No. 2012-002.
concerned personnel to properly coordinate in the preparation of various reports for consistency of information.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
direct the MPDC to appraise the socio-economic and environmental conditions of the locality and identify the programs and activities to be given priority to address the necessity of the constituents.
direct the MPDC and the Municipal Engineer to see to it that the projects to be charged against the 20% Development Fund are in line with the allowable development projects enumerated in the DILG and DBM JMC No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 amended by JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.
instruct the Municipal Accountant to carefully prepare the List of Vouchers Against the Fund to accurately present the relevant information.
direct the concerned personnel to properly coordinate in the preparation of various reports for consistency of information.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to:
strictly comply with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184;
judiciously check the documents submitted by the bidders; and
submit to the Audit Team the copies of Purchase Orders and the supporting documents within the period prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor strictly comply with RA 9344 especially on the budgetary requirement.
Also, we recommended for the submission of a detailed budget for the LCPC.

1.6	We recommended that the Inventory Committee be directed to finish the conduct of physical count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), reconcile the Accounting and Treasury property records and immediately submit the Report of the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) to the Office of the Auditor.

2.5	We recommended that the Management employ measure to ensure compliance in the timely submission of the Sources and Utilization of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund as required under Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012 – 002. Moreover, we recommend that the LDRRM Officer coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to prepare the said report on monthly basis.

3.6	We recommended that Management allocate in their budget the 1% budgetary requirement for the LCPC program as provided for in Section 15 of RA 9144 to ensure the successful implementation of the PPAs enumerated in Item II of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120.

4.5	We recommended that Management prepare a 5-Year Business Plan for every Local Economic Enterprises (LEE) to provide a clear direction of the operation aimed towards financial viability and sustainability.

5.6	We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the guidelines set under Sections 37 of Republic Act 9003 in order to deliver to its constituents the maximum benefits of which this Act is created.

6.7 We recommended Management to direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the intra-agency receivables and intra-payables accounts and effect adjustments to reflect the correct account balances thereby ensuring fair presentation on the financial reports of the LGU.

7.5	We recommended that Management adhere to the provisions of Section 3.0 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2016-01 by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA and facilitate the conduct of gender analysis using the HGDG tool for proper identification of gender-responsive PPAs through its GAD Focal Point System.

8.5	We recommended Management to direct the Municipal Accountant to document the request for write-off of the dormant accounts and strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular 2016-005 to ensure fair presentation of the account balances in the financial statements.
We recommended that the officials and employees ensure that the identified programs/projects are those mentioned under DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 and Section 287 of RA 7160.
